entry.title: The Hard Problem of Consciousness — Why the Brain Is Not Enough

link.title: The Hard Problem of Consciousness — Why the Brain Is Not Enough

entry.content:
There is a question that will not go away.

You can build the most complete neuroscience imaginable — map every neuron, every synapse, every firing pattern, every electrochemical cascade in the human brain. You can explain perception, memory, attention, emotion, language, decision-making, sleep, pain.

And when you are done — when the map is complete — you will still not have answered this:

Why is there something it is like to be you?

Why does the processing feel like anything at all? Why, when light of wavelength 700nm strikes your retina, is there a redness — a vivid, ineffable, interior redness — rather than just information processing in the dark?

Why is the brain not just a very sophisticated calculator — doing everything it does, producing all the behavior it produces — in total darkness, without experience, without anyone home?

This is the Hard Problem of Consciousness.

It is the most difficult question in philosophy. It may be the most difficult question that has ever been asked.

I. David Chalmers and the Problem’s Name

The term Hard Problem was introduced by the Australian philosopher David Chalmers in his 1995 paper “Facing Up to the Problem of Consciousness” and developed in his book The Conscious Mind (1996).

Chalmers distinguished between the easy problems and the hard problem.

The easy problems — he acknowledged they are not actually easy — are questions about the functions of consciousness:

How does the brain integrate information from different senses? How does it direct attention? How does it produce verbal reports about mental states? How does it regulate sleep and wakefulness? How does it control behavior?

These are difficult scientific questions. But they are, in principle, tractable — we can imagine what a complete answer would look like. A complete answer would be a functional account: here is the mechanism that performs this function.

The hard problem is different in kind.

Even if we answered every easy problem — even if we had a complete functional account of every cognitive capacity — we would still face this:

Why does the performance of these functions feel like anything at all?

Why is there subjective experience? Why is there something it is like to see red, to feel pain, to hear music, to be afraid, to be in love?

A complete functional account tells you what the system does. It does not tell you why the doing is accompanied by experience.

II. Qualia — The Raw Feel of Experience

The philosophical term for the subjective qualities of experience is qualia (singular: quale).

The redness of red. The painfulness of pain. The taste of coffee. The sound of middle C on a piano. The feeling of velvet under your fingertips.

These are qualia — the “what it is like” of experience, the raw, irreducible feel that seems to be left over after every functional description has been given.

The philosopher Frank Jackson introduced one of the most famous thought experiments in philosophy of mind:

Mary’s Room.

Mary is a brilliant neuroscientist who has lived her entire life in a black and white room. She has learned everything there is to know about the physics and neuroscience of color vision — every wavelength, every receptor, every neural pathway, every functional role of color perception.

She knows everything physical there is to know about seeing red.

Then she leaves the room. She sees a red tomato for the first time.

Does she learn something new?

Jackson’s intuition — shared by most people when they first encounter the thought experiment — is yes. She learns what it is like to see red. She acquires knowledge of the quale — the subjective redness — that no amount of physical knowledge gave her.

If this is right — if Mary learns something new — then there are facts about consciousness that are not captured by physical facts.

This is the knowledge argument for the irreducibility of consciousness.

III. Nagel and the Bat

In 1974, the philosopher Thomas Nagel published a paper that changed philosophy of mind forever:

“What Is It Like to Be a Bat?”

Bats navigate by echolocation — emitting high-frequency sounds and perceiving the echoes. This is a sense we do not have.

We can know everything about bat echolocation — the physics of the sound waves, the anatomy of the bat’s auditory system, the neural processing of echo information.

But can we know what it is like to be a bat? Can we know the subjective experience of perceiving the world through echolocation?

Nagel’s answer: no. Not because we lack information — but because the subjective character of experience cannot be captured by any objective description, no matter how complete.

Objectivity, by definition, abstracts away from the particular point of view. But experience is essentially perspectival — it exists only from a point of view.

“An organism has conscious mental states if and only if there is something it is like to be that organism — some subjective character to its experience.”

This formulation — “what it is like” — became the standard philosophical shorthand for the hard problem.

It points to something real and important: the distinction between third-person, objective description and first-person, subjective experience — and the difficulty of bridging this gap.

IV. The Explanatory Gap

In 1983, Joseph Levine identified what he called the explanatory gap.

Even if we discovered that pain is identical to C-fiber stimulation — even if neuroscience established a complete neural correlate for every conscious state — there would remain an explanatory gap:

We could not explain why C-fiber stimulation feels like pain rather than pleasure, or feels like anything at all.

The identity might be true. But it would be a brute identity — unexplained, unjustified, a coincidence at the level of fundamental facts.

Compare this to other scientific reductions:

Heat is mean molecular kinetic energy. This identity is not just asserted — it is explained. We understand why increased molecular motion produces the phenomenon we call heat. The explanation closes the gap.

Water is H₂O. This identity is explained — the properties of water follow from the properties of hydrogen and oxygen bonds.

But pain is C-fiber stimulation?

Even if true — we cannot explain why C-fiber stimulation should feel like anything, let alone why it should feel like this.

The gap remains.

V. Physicalist Responses

The hard problem has generated a vast literature of responses, rescues, and rejections.

The physicalist — who holds that everything is physical — has several options:

Type B Physicalism / A Posteriori Identity: Consciousness is identical to physical states, but this identity is discoverable only empirically, not a priori. Just as we discovered a posteriori that water is H₂O, we will discover that consciousness is some neural process. The explanatory gap is an epistemic gap — a gap in our knowledge — not an ontological gap in reality.

Eliminativism (Churchland): Consciousness as we conceive it — qualia, subjective experience — is a folk psychological concept that will be eliminated by mature neuroscience, the way phlogiston was eliminated by chemistry. There are no qualia because our concept of qualia is confused.

Illusionism (Frankish): Qualia seem to have intrinsic, non-physical properties — but this seeming is itself a cognitive illusion. We are systematically mistaken about the nature of our own experience. The hard problem is hard because we are trying to explain an illusion that seems like a reality.

Functionalism: Mental states are defined by their functional roles — by what they do, not what they are made of. If a system performs the right functions, it is conscious. The hard problem dissolves because consciousness just is functional organization.

Each of these responses has serious problems.

Eliminativism seems to prove too much — the existence of experience is the one thing that seems undeniable. Descartes’s cogito survives everything: even if I am deceived about everything else, there is experience of being deceived.

Illusionism faces what Chalmers calls the “meta-problem” — even if qualia are illusory, we need to explain why they seem to be non-physical. And that seeming is itself a conscious phenomenon that requires explanation.

Functionalism faces the zombie argument.

VI. Philosophical Zombies

Chalmers’s most provocative contribution to the hard problem debate is the conceivability argument via philosophical zombies.

A philosophical zombie (p-zombie) is a being that is physically and functionally identical to a human — atom for atom, neuron for neuron, behavior for behavior — but has no subjective experience.

The lights are on — the behavior is there — but nobody is home.

Chalmers argues: p-zombies are conceivable. We can coherently imagine a world physically identical to ours in which all human behavior occurs but without any accompanying experience.

If p-zombies are conceivable, they are (in some sense) possible.

If they are possible — then consciousness is not entailed by physical facts. Because the physical facts are all the same in the zombie world, but consciousness is absent.

Therefore: consciousness is not a physical fact. Therefore: physicalism is false.

The argument is controversial. Critics argue that conceivability does not entail possibility — we can conceive of things that are metaphysically impossible.

But the argument captures something important: there seems to be no logical contradiction in the idea of a physically complete world without experience.

Why does experience exist at all? Why is there something rather than nothing — not in the cosmological sense, but in the experiential sense?

VII. Panpsychism — The Unexpected Comeback

If consciousness cannot be explained by physical processes — if it cannot be reduced, eliminated, or functionalized away — one response is to build it into the foundations.

Panpsychism — the view that consciousness is a fundamental feature of reality, present at every level of physical organization — has experienced a remarkable revival in academic philosophy of mind.

Philosophers like Galen Strawson, Philip Goff, and (in a different form) David Chalmers have argued that panpsychism may be the most coherent response to the hard problem.

The basic argument: we know that consciousness exists — it is the one thing we cannot doubt. We know that it is correlated with physical processes. If we cannot explain how physical processes give rise to consciousness, perhaps consciousness is not something that arises — perhaps it is already there, in some primitive form, at the level of fundamental physics.

Electrons have mass, charge, spin — and perhaps something like experience, infinitely simpler than human experience, but present.

This is not the claim that electrons are conscious in the way humans are conscious. It is the claim that the capacity for experience is a fundamental feature of reality — as fundamental as mass or charge.

The combination problem is the main challenge: how do micro-experiences combine to produce the unified, rich experience of human consciousness?

This is unsolved. But panpsychists argue it is more tractable than the hard problem itself — explaining how experiences combine is easier than explaining how experience arises from non-experience.

VIII. Integrated Information Theory

The neuroscientist Giulio Tononi proposed Integrated Information Theory (IIT) as a mathematical framework for consciousness.

The central claim: consciousness is identical to integrated information — a property of systems that is high when the system processes information in a way that cannot be decomposed into independent parts.

The measure of integrated information is called Φ (phi).

A system with high Φ has high consciousness. A system with low Φ — like a grid of independent pixels — has low consciousness. A system with Φ = 0 has no consciousness.

IIT makes surprising predictions: a simple feedback circuit might have more consciousness than a much more complex feedforward system. The internet, despite its complexity, might be nearly unconscious — because its processing is highly decomposable. The human cerebellum, despite having more neurons than the cortex, might contribute less to consciousness — because its architecture is more modular.

IIT has been criticized from multiple directions: it seems to imply that certain simple systems have more consciousness than complex AI systems — which strikes many as counterintuitive. Its mathematical foundations have been questioned. It has been called “unfalsifiable” by some critics.

But it represents a serious attempt to bring the hard problem within reach of science — to give consciousness a mathematical foothold in the physical world.

IX. Global Workspace Theory

Bernard Baars proposed Global Workspace Theory (GWT) as a cognitive architecture for consciousness.

The basic idea: the brain contains many specialized, unconscious processors — for vision, audition, motor control, memory, emotion.

Consciousness arises when information is broadcast to a global workspace — a centralized system that makes information available to all the specialized processors simultaneously.

When you become conscious of something, it enters the global workspace — it becomes globally available, influencing and being influenced by all the other processors.

GWT has been developed and formalized by Stanislas Dehaene into Global Neuronal Workspace Theory — with specific neural predictions about the brain regions and dynamics associated with conscious access.

GWT is a theory of access consciousness — of when information is available for report and control. Critics argue it does not address phenomenal consciousness — the felt quality of experience.

In Chalmers’s terms: GWT solves an easy problem — the functional problem of conscious access — but leaves the hard problem untouched.

Why does global broadcast feel like anything?

X. The Hard Problem and Physics

Here is the deepest version of the hard problem.

Quantum mechanics — as we discussed elsewhere on this blog — has a measurement problem.

The wavefunction evolves deterministically until measurement, when it collapses to a definite state.

Some interpretations — von Neumann, Wigner — assign a special role to the observer. Collapse occurs when the measurement enters consciousness.

This is not the mainstream view. But it has never been entirely refuted.

If consciousness plays a role in quantum mechanics — if the observer is not just a physical system but a conscious one — then physics and the hard problem are not separate.

Roger Penrose and Stuart Hameroff proposed Orchestrated Objective Reduction (Orch-OR): consciousness arises from quantum computations in microtubules — protein structures inside neurons — and is connected to fundamental physics through a form of objective wavefunction collapse.

This theory is controversial — most neuroscientists find it implausible. But it represents a serious attempt to connect the hard problem to the foundations of physics — to argue that consciousness is not an afterthought but is written into the structure of reality at the deepest level.

XI. Why It Matters

The hard problem is not an academic puzzle.

It touches everything.

Ethics: If consciousness is the basis of moral status — if what matters morally is whether there is something it is like to be an entity — then the hard problem determines who and what deserves moral consideration.

Is a newborn conscious? When does consciousness begin? Is an animal in pain conscious? Is a brain-damaged patient in a vegetative state conscious? Is a future AI system conscious?

We cannot answer these questions without a theory of consciousness.

Medicine: Disorders of consciousness — coma, vegetative state, minimally conscious state — require us to determine whether a patient is conscious from the outside. The hard problem is the theoretical background of this clinical problem.

Artificial Intelligence: As AI systems become more sophisticated — as they produce more human-like outputs, demonstrate more flexible behavior — the question of AI consciousness becomes urgent.

Is GPT conscious? Is there something it is like to process a prompt? Without a solution to the hard problem, we cannot answer this.

And the answer matters — morally, practically, existentially.

XII. Where We Are

We do not know why there is experience.

We know an enormous amount about the neural correlates of consciousness — which brain regions are active during various conscious states, how anesthesia disrupts consciousness, how damage to specific regions produces specific disruptions of experience.

We have theories — IIT, GWT, Orch-OR, higher-order theories, global workspace, predictive processing.

We do not have an explanation of why any physical process should be accompanied by experience.

Chalmers, in 1995, hoped the problem would be solved within decades. Thirty years later, it has not been solved. If anything, it has become clearer how difficult it is — clearer that the easy problems and the hard problem are genuinely different in kind.

Francis Crick, who co-discovered the structure of DNA, spent the last decades of his life working on consciousness. He died without solving it.

William James — who wrote the first great psychology textbook in 1890 — said that the relationship between brain and consciousness was “the most mysterious thing in the world.”

It still is.

Coda

You are reading this sentence.

Behind your eyes, something is happening — not just processing, not just computation, but experience.

There is a redness somewhere, a sense of the words meaning something, a quality of attention.

This — this very thing, happening right now — is what the hard problem is about.

Not a distant philosophical puzzle. Not a question about other minds.

This. Now. The fact that there is something rather than nothing.

The fact that you are here, and that being here feels like something.

We do not know why.

That is either the most disturbing fact in the universe —

or the most miraculous one.

Probably both.

Further reading: David Chalmers — The Conscious Mind (1996) Thomas Nagel — Mortal Questions (1979) — contains “What Is It Like to Be a Bat?” Frank Jackson — “Epiphenomenal Qualia” (1982) Daniel Dennett — Consciousness Explained (1991) — the best physicalist response Philip Goff — Galileo’s Error (2019) — the best introduction to panpsychism Stanislas Dehaene — Consciousness and the Brain (2014) Antonio Damasio — The Feeling of What Happens (1999)
entry.summary: On qualia, the explanatory gap, and the most difficult question in all of philosophy and science.

entry.title: Plato — The Cave, the Sun, and the Man Who Chose to Die

link.title: Plato — The Cave, the Sun, and the Man Who Chose to Die

entry.content:
There is a cave.

Inside the cave, prisoners sit chained — facing a wall. Behind them, a fire burns. Between the fire and the prisoners, people walk carrying objects — statues of animals, figures, things.

The shadows of these objects fall on the wall.

The prisoners have been here since birth. They have never seen the fire. They have never seen the objects. They have seen only the shadows.

For them, the shadows are reality. They have names for the shadows. They have expertise in predicting which shadow will appear next. They compete with each other in this expertise. They are proud of what they know.

Then one prisoner is freed.

He turns. He sees the fire — blinding, painful. He is dragged upward, out of the cave, into the sunlight.

He cannot see at first. Everything is too bright, too real, too much. Gradually, he adjusts. He sees the actual objects. He sees the actual world. He sees, finally, the sun itself — the source of all light, all visibility, all truth.

He goes back into the cave.

He tries to tell the others what he has seen.

They do not believe him. His eyes, readjusted to the dark, can no longer predict the shadows as well as they once could.

They think the journey has made him stupid.

If they could, they would kill him.

I. Plato and the Shape of Western Thought

Plato was born in Athens around 428 BCE.

Alfred North Whitehead, the mathematician and philosopher, wrote that the history of Western philosophy is “a series of footnotes to Plato.”

This is not hyperbole. It is barely metaphor.

Every major question Western philosophy has asked — What is real? What is knowledge? What is justice? What is beauty? What is the good life? How should we organize society? What is the relationship between the particular and the universal? — Plato asked first, and answered with a systematic completeness that has shaped the terms of debate ever since.

He did not merely ask questions. He invented the dialogue as a philosophical form — philosophy as conversation, as the encounter between minds, as the process of thinking together rather than the delivery of conclusions.

He founded the Academy — the first institution of higher learning in the Western world — which continued for over nine hundred years, until the Emperor Justinian closed it in 529 CE.

And he had a teacher whose death shaped everything he wrote.

II. Socrates — The Man Who Knew Nothing

Socrates wrote nothing.

Everything we know about him comes from others — primarily Plato, but also Xenophon and Aristophanes. The Socrates of Plato’s dialogues may or may not be the historical Socrates — scholars have debated this for centuries.

What is clear: Socrates was a man who walked the streets of Athens asking questions.

Not rhetorical questions. Not questions to which he had answers. Questions he genuinely did not know the answer to — or claimed not to.

He asked generals what courage was. He asked politicians what justice was. He asked poets what beauty was.

In every case, the person he asked began with confidence and ended in confusion — their assumed knowledge dissolved by the patient, relentless pressure of Socratic questioning.

This method — elenchus, refutation — was not designed to humiliate. It was designed to reveal: that what we think we know, we often do not know at all.

The Oracle at Delphi had declared Socrates the wisest man in Athens. Socrates investigated this claim by speaking to people assumed to be wise. His conclusion:

“I know that I know nothing — and in this, I am wiser than those who think they know but do not.”

This is the beginning of philosophy: not the possession of answers but the honest confrontation with ignorance.

The Athenians found this intolerable.

III. The Allegory of the Cave — A Close Reading

The Allegory of the Cave appears in Book VII of The Republic — Plato’s most ambitious work, a dialogue about justice that becomes a dialogue about everything.

The cave is not a metaphor for ignorance in the simple sense — for not knowing things. It is a metaphor for the structure of ordinary consciousness — for the way we all, inevitably, mistake appearance for reality, shadow for substance.

The prisoners are not stupid. They are us.

Their mistake is not a failure of intelligence — it is a failure of position. They are chained in a way that prevents them from seeing the sources of what they see. They see effects without causes, appearances without the things that generate appearances.

The shadows on the wall correspond, in Plato’s framework, to the world of ordinary experience — the world of particular, changing, impermanent things that we perceive through our senses.

The objects carried in front of the fire correspond to the mathematical and scientific objects — more real than the shadows, but still dependent on the fire (the intellect) that illuminates them.

The sunlit world outside the cave corresponds to the realm of the Forms — the perfect, eternal, unchanging archetypes of which everything in the sensory world is an imperfect copy.

The sun itself corresponds to the Form of the Good — the highest Form, the source of all being and all knowability, which Plato identifies, in some passages, with God.

This is the Theory of Forms — Plato’s most radical and most contested contribution to philosophy.

IV. The Theory of Forms

Consider a circle.

You have seen many circles — drawn on paper, traced in sand, formed by bent wire. None of them is a perfect circle. Every drawn circle has imperfections — slight irregularities, a width to the line, a material instantiation that falls short of the ideal.

But you know what a perfect circle is. You can recognize that drawn circles approximate it. You can measure how far they deviate from it.

Where is this perfect circle? Not in the physical world — it has no physical instantiation. Not in any mind — it is not a subjective impression.

For Plato: it exists in the realm of Forms — a non-physical, non-mental realm of perfect, eternal objects that the physical world imperfectly instantiates.

The Form of Circle is more real than any drawn circle — because it is what all drawn circles are trying to be, what they participate in, what makes them circles rather than squiggles.

Extend this to everything:

The Form of Beauty — of which beautiful things are imperfect copies. The Form of Justice — of which just acts are imperfect instantiations. The Form of the Good — the highest Form, which illuminates all other Forms the way the sun illuminates the physical world.

Mathematical objects — numbers, geometric figures — are the clearest examples of Forms. Seven is not a physical object. It is not in anyone’s head. It is necessarily what it is — eternally, changelessly. It is more real, in Plato’s sense, than any collection of seven apples.

The philosopher’s task — the freed prisoner’s task — is to turn away from the shadows, to follow the difficult path upward, to achieve knowledge of the Forms, and ultimately to contemplate the Good itself.

V. Why the Philosopher Must Return

The freed prisoner goes back.

This is the move that distinguishes Plato’s philosopher from the mystic or the pure contemplative.

Having seen the sun — having achieved the highest knowledge — the philosopher does not remain in the light. They return to the cave. They try to govern, to educate, to lead.

Not because the cave is pleasant — it is not, and they will be mocked and perhaps killed for their trouble.

But because justice requires it.

In The Republic, the philosopher-king — the person who has achieved knowledge of the Good — has an obligation to use that knowledge in the service of the community.

The highest knowledge is not private. It is political. It creates obligation.

This is one of the most contested claims in Plato.

Some readers find it authoritarian: who decides who the philosopher-kings are? Who guards the guardians?

Others find it the only coherent answer to the problem of political legitimacy: rule should be in the hands of those who know — not those who are wealthy, or popular, or born to the right family, but those who have genuinely understood the Good.

The tension is not resolved in The Republic. It is not resolved in political philosophy generally. It is one of the permanent problems of the relationship between knowledge and power.

VI. The Death of Socrates

In 399 BCE, Socrates was put on trial.

The charges: impiety (not believing in the gods of the city) and corrupting the youth of Athens.

The trial was political — Socrates had been associated with figures on the losing side of the recent civil conflict, including Critias, one of the brutal Thirty Tyrants.

The jury: 501 Athenian citizens. The vote: 280 to 221 for conviction.

He was given the opportunity to propose an alternative penalty.

He proposed that Athens should pay him a salary for his service to the city.

The jury, unamused, voted for death: 360 to 141.

He was sentenced to drink hemlock — a poison that causes progressive paralysis, working upward from the feet.

He had the opportunity to escape. His friends arranged it. He refused.

The Crito — one of Plato’s most powerful dialogues — records his reasons.

He had lived his entire life in Athens. He had accepted the benefits of Athenian law. To escape now would be to violate the implicit contract between a citizen and the laws of their city — to act as if the laws apply only when convenient, which would destroy the entire basis of law.

He would not do it.

He drank the hemlock.

The Phaedo records his last hours — a philosophical conversation with his friends about the immortality of the soul.

Socrates argues, with his characteristic combination of intellectual rigor and personal serenity, that the philosopher should not fear death — that the entire philosophical life is a preparation for death, a practice of separating the soul from the body, of prioritizing the eternal over the temporary.

His last words, to his friend Crito:

“Crito, we owe a cock to Asclepius. Pay it and do not neglect it.”

Asclepius was the god of healing. A cock was offered to him in gratitude for recovery from illness.

Socrates, dying, was expressing gratitude — for recovery from the illness of life, for the healing that death brings to the soul that has always sought truth.

Whether you find this beautiful or chilling depends on what you think about the soul.

What is undeniable: the man died as he had lived — without fear, without pretense, attending to the small obligations even at the last moment.

VII. What the Cave Gets Right

The Allegory of the Cave is not science. The Theory of Forms is not, in any straightforward sense, true — at least not in the way Plato meant it.

But the cave gets something permanently right.

We do not see reality directly.

We see appearances — filtered through our senses, our concepts, our culture, our language, our evolutionary history, our neurological architecture.

The history of science is the history of discovering that what seems obvious is not — that the sun does not go around the earth, that solid matter is mostly empty space, that time is not absolute, that the observer affects the observed.

The cave metaphor captures something that naive realism — the view that we simply see the world as it is — cannot account for.

Education is not the filling of an empty vessel.

Plato’s image of education — the turning of the whole person toward the light — is still the most profound account of what genuine learning requires.

Not the acquisition of information. The reorientation of attention, the development of new capacities for seeing, the willingness to be disoriented by what is more real than what you were accustomed to.

Knowledge creates obligation.

The philosopher who returns to the cave — this image of knowledge as the beginning of responsibility rather than its exemption — remains one of the most powerful arguments against the view that intellectual life is purely private.

If you see more clearly, you owe something to those who do not yet see.

What you owe, exactly, is the hardest question.

VIII. The Permanent Socratic Question

Socrates did not die for a doctrine. He did not die for a theory of Forms or a proof of the soul’s immortality.

He died for a practice — the practice of examining life, of refusing to accept unexamined assumptions, of following an argument wherever it leads regardless of the social cost.

“The unexamined life is not worth living.”

This sentence — from the Apology, Socrates’s speech at his trial — is the most provocative sentence in Western philosophy.

Not because it is obviously true. Because it is obviously debatable — and the debate it provokes is itself an instance of the examined life it commends.

Is the examined life better than the unexamined one? What counts as examination? Is the peasant who never reads philosophy but lives with integrity and love living an unexamined life?

Socrates would have enjoyed these questions. He would have had no final answers.

He would have kept asking.

That, for him, was the point.

IX. Plato’s Shadow on Everything

Every time you distinguish appearance from reality, you are doing something Platonic.

Every time you argue that there is a right answer — not just a popular one, not just a useful one, but a genuinely correct one — you are implicitly committed to something like the Forms.

Every time you believe that mathematics discovers truths rather than inventing them — that the Pythagorean theorem was true before anyone proved it — you are a Platonist in mathematics.

Every time you feel that justice is not merely what the powerful decide — that there is a standard against which even the powerful can be judged — you are reaching for something like the Form of Justice.

Plato is not a historical curiosity. He is the structure of certain ways of thinking that we have not yet found a way to abandon — because they capture something about how we actually engage with truth, with value, with the world.

Even those who reject him most forcefully — Aristotle, Nietzsche, the logical positivists — are defined by their rejection. You cannot escape Plato. You can only choose your relationship to him.

Start with the Apology — Socrates’s speech at his trial. Then the Phaedo — his last hours. Then the Republic — if you have the patience for it, it will repay you beyond almost any book I know. The Allegory of the Cave is Book VII — but read Books I through VI first. The cave means more when you know what came before it.
entry.summary: On the Allegory of the Cave, the Theory of Forms, and why Socrates drank the hemlock.

entry.title: Bivariate Bicycle Codes — A New Hope for qLDPC

link.title: Bivariate Bicycle Codes — A New Hope for qLDPC

entry.content:
qLDPC codes have been theoretically promising for years. The question was always: can we find explicit constructions with good parameters and tractable decoding and some hope of physical implementation?

Bivariate Bicycle (BB) codes, introduced by Bravyi et al. (2024), are a serious answer to that question.

Construction

BB codes are defined over the group algebra of \(\mathbb{Z}_l \times \mathbb{Z}_m\).

Let \(x\) and \(y\) be the cyclic shift operators on \(\mathbb{Z}_l\) and \(\mathbb{Z}_m\) respectively. Choose two polynomials:

\[A(x,y), \quad B(x,y) \in \mathbb{F}_2[x,y] / (x^l - 1, y^m - 1)\]

The parity check matrices are:

\[H_X = [A \mid B], \qquad H_Z = [B^T \mid A^T]\]

This gives a CSS code on \(n = 2lm\) physical qubits.

The commutativity condition \(H_X H_Z^T = 0\) is automatically satisfied by construction — a clean algebraic consequence of the bicyclic structure.

Why BB Codes Are Interesting

1. High encoding rate.

Surface code encodes \(k=1\) logical qubit into \(n \sim d^2\) physical qubits. BB codes achieve \(k = 2 \cdot \text{rank}(\ker A \cap \ker B^T)\), which for well-chosen polynomials gives \(k \sim \Theta(n)\).

The landmark example from Bravyi et al.: [[144, 12, 12]] — 144 physical qubits encoding 12 logical qubits at distance 12. Surface code needs ~1728 physical qubits for the same parameters.

2. Low-weight stabilizers.

All stabilizers have weight exactly 6 — three from \(A\), three from \(B\). This is a hard constraint from the polynomial structure, and it’s what makes BB codes physically plausible. Compare to hypergraph product codes where stabilizer weights grow with \(n\).

3. Quasi-cyclic structure.

The cyclic structure enables efficient syndrome extraction circuits and makes BP-OSD decoding particularly effective on these codes.

The Polynomial Choice Problem

Here’s what Bravyi et al. didn’t fully answer:

Given a group \(\mathbb{Z}_l \times \mathbb{Z}_m\), which polynomial pairs \((A, B)\) maximize the distance \(d\)?

Shaw & Terhal (2024) explored this more systematically, introducing the morphing circuit framework and identifying high-performance BB codes in several group sizes.

But their search was not exhaustive. The polynomial space is large, and distance computation is expensive — exact distance for a [[72, 12, ?]] code requires solving a minimum-weight codeword problem that is NP-hard in general.

So the question remains open: Are there BB codes with better parameters than currently known?

What I’m Working On

Systematic search in groups Shaw & Terhal didn’t fully explore.

For \(\mathbb{Z}_{12} \times \mathbb{Z}_6\) — \(n = 144\) physical qubits — I’ve been running exhaustive searches over polynomial pairs that include cross-terms: \(x^i y^j\) monomials Shaw restricted away.

Early results: several [[72, 12, 7]] codes with polynomial choices outside Shaw’s search space.

The trade-off: cross-term polynomials yield stabilizer weight 11 instead of Shaw’s weight 9 — more CNOT gates, harder circuits. Whether the distance gain justifies the circuit cost depends on the physical error model.

That’s the next question.

More soon. 🙂
entry.summary: Structure, parameters, and why they matter.

entry.title: Chingiz Aitmatov — The Writer Who Carried a Whole World on His Back

link.title: Chingiz Aitmatov — The Writer Who Carried a Whole World on His Back

entry.content:
There is a writer whose name most Western readers do not know.

He was born in 1928 in a small village in Kyrgyzstan — a place so remote that the nearest railway station was a day’s journey away. He wrote in both Kyrgyz and Russian. He was translated into over 170 languages. He sold hundreds of millions of copies worldwide.

In the Soviet Union, he was the most widely read living author. Gabriel García Márquez called him one of the greatest writers of the century. Mikhail Gorbachev considered him a personal friend and advisor.

His name was Chingiz Aitmatov.

If you have not read him, you have a magnificent gift waiting for you.

I. The World He Came From

To read Aitmatov without understanding where he came from is to read him with one eye closed.

Kyrgyzstan — the land between the Tian Shan mountains and the vast steppes of Central Asia — is one of the oldest inhabited places on earth, and one of the least known to Western readers.

The Kyrgyz people were nomads for millennia — their culture organized around movement, horses, the sky, the relationship between the human and the elemental. Their oral tradition — the Epic of Manas, the longest epic poem in any language, three times the length of the Odyssey and the Iliad combined — carried the memory of the people across centuries of conquest and transformation.

Then came the Russians. Then the Soviets.

Collectivization destroyed the nomadic way of life. The great Manaschi — the epic singers who had memorized hundreds of thousands of verses — were classified as class enemies. The old language, the old stories, the old relationship with the land — all of it was being systematically dismantled.

Aitmatov grew up in the ruins of this dismantling.

His father, Törökul Aitmatov — a Communist Party official and intellectual — was arrested in 1937 during Stalin’s purges and shot the following year. Chingiz was nine years old.

His mother raised four children alone in a village with no men — they had all been taken by the purges or the war. At fourteen, Aitmatov was the village tax collector. At fifteen, he was secretary of the village council.

He carried the weight of adults before he was done being a child.

This is not background. This is the source. Everything Aitmatov wrote came from this — from knowing what it means to lose a father to the state, to watch a culture be erased, to carry too much too soon, to love a landscape that is being destroyed.

II. The White Ship — Белый пароход (1970)

There is a boy.

He is seven years old, perhaps eight. He lives with his grandfather Momun in a remote forest ranger’s post in the mountains of Kyrgyzstan. His parents are gone — separated, absent, unreachable. He has a school bag. He treasures it.

He has three friends: a pair of binoculars through which he observes the distant world, a stone in the shape of a fish, and a white ship he can see far away on the lake below.

He has invented a game: he will turn into a fish, swim down the river to the lake, find the white ship, and tell his father — who he believes is on that ship — everything that has happened.

The boy lives in two worlds simultaneously: the world of the forest ranger’s post — with its adult cruelties, its compromises, its small corruptions — and the world of myth, the world of the Horned Mother Deer who, according to Kyrgyz legend, brought the ancestors of the Kyrgyz people back to life after they were exterminated by enemies.

The Horned Mother Deer is the spirit of the forest, the principle of regeneration, the promise that destruction is not the final word.

Grandfather Momun tells the boy this story. The boy believes it — not naively, but with the full force of a child’s capacity for mythic truth.

Then Orozkul — the boy’s uncle by marriage, the man who controls the post, brutal, drunk, resentful — kills a deer.

Not just a deer. The last deer in the forest. In violation of every prohibition, every law, every mythic interdiction the grandfather has taught the boy.

And Momun — gentle, compromised, afraid of Orozkul — helps him do it.

The boy watches his grandfather butcher the deer. The man who told him the myth participates in the myth’s destruction.

There is no rescue. No redemption. No return of the Horned Mother Deer.

The boy walks into the river.

“He did not want to live as a human being anymore. He wanted to be a fish and swim away.”

What The White Ship Does

Aitmatov said in an interview that he agonized over the ending.

His editors wanted him to change it — to give the boy a rescue, a hope, a future. Soviet literature required optimism. The Party required that stories end with forward motion, with the possibility of the new Soviet man.

Aitmatov refused.

The ending is not a celebration of death. It is a statement about what happens when the sources of meaning — myth, story, the grandfather’s love — are corrupted from within.

The boy does not die because the world is cruel. He dies because the person who gave him the myth helped destroy it.

This is the specific, precise, unbearable betrayal at the center of the novel: not the cruelty of enemies but the compromise of love.

Momun loves the boy. Momun is also weak. These two things are simultaneously true and their simultaneous truth is what breaks everything.

The novel is short — a novella, really. It can be read in an afternoon.

It will stay with you for decades.

III. The Day Lasts More Than a Hundred Years

И дольше века длится день (1980)

This is Aitmatov’s masterpiece. This is, I will say without qualification, one of the great novels of the twentieth century — and one of the least known in the West.

The title comes from a Pasternak poem: “February. Get ink and weep…” The full line: “And the longer a day lasts, the longer it seems to last.” The novel’s Russian title is more literal: The Day Lasts Longer Than a Century.

The novel takes place over a single day and night — the day that Yedigei Zhangel’din, a worker at a small railway junction in the Kazakh steppe, must bury his lifelong friend Kazangap.

But the single day contains a century.

Yedigei’s journey to the burial ground — across the vast, flat steppe, on a camel named Karanar — unravels into memory: his entire life, his friendship with Kazangap, the history of the junction, the history of the steppe, the history of the Kazakh people.

The railway junction is called Boranly-Burannyi — the Windy Place, the Place of Blizzards. Trains pass through it from east to west and west to east, carrying everything and everyone, connecting and separating simultaneously.

“Here in this place the trains went from east to west and from west to east.”

This line — deceptively simple — is one of the most resonant geographical metaphors in modern literature. The steppe as crossroads. The junction as the point where all directions meet and none is home.

Yedigei has lived here for decades — arrived after the war, stayed, raised children, buried friends, watched the world change while the steppe remained.

He is not a hero in any conventional sense. He is a man of the steppe — patient, sturdy, with a memory that holds everything and a grief that cannot be expressed quickly.

The Mankurt

Woven through the novel is the legend of the Mankurt.

In Aitmatov’s retelling of the ancient Zhuan legend:

Enemy warriors, when they captured prisoners, would sometimes perform a procedure designed to destroy the prisoner’s memory — to make them forget their name, their family, their language, their identity.

The procedure: the prisoner’s head was shaved, then encased in a raw camel hide. The hide was placed in the desert sun. As it dried and shrank, it constricted — causing unbearable pain, sometimes killing, sometimes driving mad.

Those who survived — those whom the hide did not kill — emerged as Mankurts: human bodies with no memory of being human. Perfect slaves. Obedient, tireless, without will or history.

They could not be freed. They did not know they were enslaved. They had no self to free.

A Mankurt, in the novel’s legend, kills his own mother when she comes to rescue him — because he does not recognize her, because the word mother means nothing to him, because the category of mother requires a self that can stand in relation to a mother — and he has no self.

Aitmatov wrote this legend in 1980 — during the height of Soviet cultural policy that systematically suppressed minority languages, traditions, histories.

The Mankurt is not a metaphor. It is a description.

A people made to forget their language are being Mankurtized. A culture whose epics are classified as dangerous is being Mankurtized. A child taught that their grandparents’ world was backward, primitive, pre-revolutionary — is being Mankurtized.

The mother in the legend — Naiman-Ana — rides to find her son, calling his name. He does not recognize her. He kills her. Her white headscarf, floating upward as she falls, becomes a white bird that flies over the steppe forever, calling: “Remember whose son you are. Remember your name.”

This image — the white bird, the headscarf, the calling — enters you and does not leave.

The Science Fiction Frame

The novel’s second narrative strand is extraordinary — and largely unknown even to people who have read the novel because it was cut from many editions.

While Yedigei makes his way across the steppe, two Soviet and American cosmonauts aboard a joint space station have made contact with an alien civilization — Lesnaya Grud (Forest Breast) — a planet of beings of extraordinary intelligence and benevolence.

The cosmic beings wish to establish contact with Earth. The Soviet and American governments, terrified of what this contact might mean — what it might reveal, what it might disrupt, what it might give their citizens — refuse.

They establish Parity — a joint space barrier around Earth to prevent any contact.

The cosmonauts, unable to comply with this order, are effectively imprisoned in space — neither able to return to Earth nor to reach the civilization that has opened itself to them.

The parallel is not subtle: the steppe and the cosmos, the burial and the barrier, the forgetting of the Mankurt and the refusal of contact — all of them are the same story.

The story of power choosing ignorance over knowledge, isolation over connection, control over the terrifying freedom of knowing more than you knew before.

IV. The Scaffold — Плаха (1986)

By 1986, Aitmatov was writing differently.

The Scaffold is his most ambitious and most uneven novel — a book that reaches for everything and does not entirely hold together, but whose moments of greatness are among the greatest things he wrote.

The novel opens — magnificently — with wolves.

Akbara and Tashchainar, a she-wolf and her mate, living in the Central Asian steppe. The wolves are not symbols. They are wolves — specific, particular, with their own perceptions and desires and fears.

Aitmatov gives us their inner life with a directness and credibility that is genuinely remarkable.

The wolves are hunting. They are raising cubs. Then the Soviet narcotics operation comes — to harvest the wild marijuana that grows on the steppe. Helicopters. Men with guns. Chaos. The herd of antelopes stampedes and is slaughtered. The wolf cubs are killed.

Akbara survives. Tashchainar survives. They lose cub after cub to human intervention — not to cruelty, exactly, but to indifference. The humans do not hate the wolves. The wolves simply do not exist in the frame of what matters to the people making the decisions.

This is the ecological and moral argument that runs through the novel: not that humans are evil but that the frame of consideration has been drawn too small — that a world in which wolves and their cubs do not count is a world in which eventually nothing will count.

Alongside the wolves: a young man searching for God in the Soviet steppe, a drug addict, a Christ figure, a party official.

The novel is not perfectly constructed. It is too long, too various, too ambitious.

But the wolves — the wolves are perfect. And the image of Akbara, having lost everything, finally taking a human child — not from malice but from a grief that has nowhere else to go — this image will not leave you.

V. The Place Connecting Two Worlds

All of Aitmatov’s novels share a geography — not literal, but mythic.

A vast, open landscape — steppe or mountain or lake — in which the human figure is small against enormous forces: time, history, the state, the cosmos.

A railway or a river or a white ship in the distance — the possibility of elsewhere, of connection, of escape.

A story within the story — a myth, a legend, a song — that contains the truth the present cannot contain directly.

And a moment of loss that is also, in some way that is not consolation but is not quite the opposite of consolation, a moment of clarity.

Aitmatov understood something about loss that most writers flinch from: that some losses are not healed, not transformed, not made meaningful. They are simply real.

The white ship does not return. The Mankurt does not remember. Akbara does not stop grieving.

But the telling — the fact that someone sat down and found the precise words for these irreversible things — this is its own kind of answer.

Not to the loss. But to the silence that loss leaves behind.

VI. Language and Double Vision

Aitmatov wrote in two languages — Kyrgyz and Russian — and this bilingualism was not incidental.

It gave him what the critic calls double vision — the ability to inhabit two cultural worlds simultaneously, to see each through the lens of the other, to find in the gap between them the space where literature lives.

His Kyrgyz gave him: the oral tradition, the epic cadences, the specific relationship between the human and the natural, the way time moves in a nomadic culture — not chronologically but in layers, the past present in the present in a way that literate cultures often suppress.

His Russian gave him: the great tradition of the Russian novel, the precision of a literary language honed by Pushkin, Tolstoy, Dostoevsky, Chekhov — the forms and structures of a literature that had been thinking about suffering and meaning for two centuries.

The result is a prose style unlike anyone else’s — vast and intimate simultaneously, moving between myth and the specific texture of daily life with a naturalness that feels inevitable in retrospect and is, in practice, extraordinarily difficult.

Magical realism is the term sometimes applied. It is not quite right.

García Márquez’s magic comes from excess — the world overfull with impossible things. Aitmatov’s myth comes from depth — the world as it is, but seen through a longer time, a deeper history, a different relationship to the sacred.

The Horned Mother Deer does not violate the laws of nature. She is the law of nature, seen from inside a tradition that has not separated the human from the natural into the opposition that modernity requires.

VII. What He Knew

Aitmatov died in 2008, in Nuremberg, of pneumonia. He was seventy-nine years old.

He had outlived the Soviet Union. He had served as Kyrgyzstan’s ambassador to several countries. He had watched his country achieve independence and struggle to understand what independence meant after centuries of being someone else’s territory.

In one of his last interviews, he was asked what he most wanted readers to take from his work.

He said: “I want them to feel that they are not alone in their grief. That their grief has been felt before. That the steppe is wide enough to hold it.”

The steppe is wide enough to hold it.

This is the sentence I return to.

Not: your grief will be healed. Not: your grief has a meaning. Not: your grief will pass.

The steppe is wide enough to hold it.

The landscape — vast, indifferent, enduring — receives what the human cannot contain. The wind takes it. The grass absorbs it. The white bird keeps flying overhead, calling a name that someone, somewhere, still remembers.

VIII. Where to Begin

If you have not read Aitmatov, begin with The White Ship.

It is the shortest, the purest, the most devastating. It will tell you immediately whether this writer’s world is your world.

For most readers who encounter it, the answer is: yes. Incomprehensibly, yes. As if the book had been waiting for you in a language you didn’t know you spoke.

Then The Day Lasts More Than a Hundred Years. Read it slowly. Let Yedigei’s journey take its time. Let the steppe do what the steppe does.

Then The Scaffold — knowing its imperfections, reading past them for the wolves.

Then Farewell, Gulsary — the story of a man and his horse, which is to say: a story about loyalty, about the relationship between the human and the animal, about what it means to grow old in a world that is growing old differently than you.

Then, when you have read them all, sit for a while with the image of a white bird flying over the steppe — a headscarf, a mother’s voice, calling a name that must not be forgotten.

“Remember whose son you are. Remember your name.”

Further reading: Chingiz Aitmatov — The White Ship (trans. Mirra Ginsburg) Chingiz Aitmatov — The Day Lasts More Than a Hundred Years (trans. John French) Chingiz Aitmatov — The Scaffold (trans. Arch Tait) Chingiz Aitmatov — Farewell, Gulsary (trans. John French) For context: Hamid Ismailov — The Railway (another Central Asian masterpiece)
entry.summary: On the most important writer you may never have read — and why that is a loss you should correct immediately.

entry.title: Decoherence — Why Quantum Computers Are Hard

link.title: Decoherence — Why Quantum Computers Are Hard

entry.content:
Last time I introduced superposition and entanglement — the two gifts quantum mechanics gives us.

Now for the curse: decoherence.

What is Decoherence?

A qubit in superposition is in a state:

\[|\psi\rangle = \alpha|0\rangle + \beta|1\rangle\]
where $$ \alpha ^2 + \beta ^2 = 1$$.

This is a coherent superposition — the amplitudes \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) have a definite phase relationship. Quantum computation exploits this phase structure through interference.

The problem: no qubit is truly isolated.

Every qubit interacts with its environment — stray electromagnetic fields, thermal photons, vibrations in the substrate, neighboring atoms. These interactions entangle the qubit with the environment.

When a qubit entangles with the environment, the reduced state of the qubit — obtained by tracing out the environmental degrees of freedom — becomes a mixed state:

\[\rho = |\alpha|^2 |0\rangle\langle 0| + |\beta|^2 |1\rangle\langle 1|\]

The off-diagonal terms — the coherences — vanish. The qubit is no longer in superposition. It has effectively been measured by the environment, without anyone asking it to be.

This is decoherence.

Two Timescales

In practice, decoherence manifests through two characteristic times:

T₁ — relaxation time: how long before the qubit loses energy and decays from |1⟩ to |0⟩. A dissipative process.

T₂ — dephasing time: how long before the phase relationship between |0⟩ and |1⟩ is destroyed, even without energy loss. Always T₂ ≤ 2T₁.

State-of-the-art superconducting qubits today achieve T₁, T₂ on the order of 100–500 microseconds.

A quantum gate takes ~10–100 nanoseconds.

So you have roughly 1000–10000 gate operations before your qubit becomes classical garbage.

For useful quantum computation — say, breaking RSA-2048 with Shor’s algorithm — you need billions of reliable gates.

The gap is enormous.

Quantum Error Correction

The solution, in principle, has been known since 1995: quantum error correction (QEC).

The idea: encode one logical qubit into many physical qubits, such that errors on individual physical qubits can be detected and corrected without ever measuring — and thus collapsing — the logical state.

The canonical example is the surface code: a 2D array of physical qubits where errors are detected by measuring stabilizers — multi-qubit Pauli operators that commute with the logical operators but anticommute with errors.

An error flips a stabilizer measurement from +1 to -1. You detect it. You correct it. The logical qubit survives.

But here’s the cost: to encode one logical qubit with surface code at a physical error rate of ~0.1%, you need roughly 1000 physical qubits.

For Shor’s algorithm on RSA-2048: ~20 million physical qubits.

We have ~1000 today.

Where I Come In

The surface code is not the only game in town.

A newer class of codes — quantum Low-Density Parity-Check (qLDPC) codes — promises dramatically better encoding rates. Instead of 1000 physical qubits per logical qubit, some qLDPC codes achieve 10–20 physical qubits per logical qubit at comparable error thresholds.

The catch: they require non-local connectivity between qubits — harder to build in hardware, more complex to decode.

This is the frontier. This is where I work.

More on that soon. 🙂
entry.summary: The fragility of quantum information and the war against noise.

entry.title: Dostoevsky — The Writer Who Knew Too Much

link.title: Dostoevsky — The Writer Who Knew Too Much

entry.content:
There is a moment in The Brothers Karamazov where Ivan Karamazov — the brilliant, tortured intellectual — hands back his ticket to God.

Not because he doesn’t believe. Because he does — and still refuses.

He has catalogued the suffering of children. Not abstract suffering. Specific cases. A five-year-old girl locked in an outhouse in winter by her parents. A serf boy torn apart by hounds for accidentally injuring a general’s dog. He recites them with the precision of a prosecutor and the anguish of a man who cannot stop seeing.

And then he says:

“It’s not God I don’t accept, it’s the world he created.”

I have read a great deal of philosophy. I have encountered the problem of evil in Leibniz, in Hume, in Mackie. None of them hit me the way Ivan hit me.

Because Dostoevsky does not argue. He shows.

The Man Himself

Fyodor Mikhailovich Dostoevsky was born in Moscow in 1821, the second of seven children of a military doctor.

He was twenty-seven years old when he was arrested for participating in the Petrashevsky Circle — a group of young intellectuals who met to discuss Fourier, socialism, and the abolition of serfdom.

He was sentenced to death.

On December 22, 1849, he was led to the Semyonovsky Square in St. Petersburg. He was bound, dressed in a white death shroud, and placed before a firing squad.

The order to fire was not given.

The execution was a staged performance — a psychological torture ordered by Tsar Nicholas I. A last-minute reprieve was read aloud. One of the men beside Dostoevsky went permanently insane.

Dostoevsky was sent to four years of hard labor in Siberia, followed by four years of compulsory military service.

He returned from Siberia a changed man.

The socialist, the Westernizer, the progressive — gone. What emerged instead was something more complex, more frightened, more profound: a man who had stared into the abyss and come back not with answers but with better questions.

The Epileptic Vision

Dostoevsky suffered from epilepsy throughout his life.

He described the aura before his seizures — a brief, ecstatic moment of total harmony and understanding — as the most intense experience he knew.

“For a few seconds I experience a happiness impossible in ordinary life. I feel complete harmony in myself and in the world, and this feeling is so strong and sweet that for a few seconds of such bliss I would give ten years of my life, perhaps my entire life.”

Then the seizure. Then darkness. Then waking, destroyed.

He gave this experience to Prince Myshkin in The Idiot. He meditated on it throughout his life.

The question it posed: what is the value of a moment of perfect truth that destroys the body experiencing it?

This is not an abstract question for Dostoevsky. It is the shape of his entire theology — the relationship between suffering and grace, between destruction and illumination.

The Underground Man

In 1864, Dostoevsky published Notes from Underground — forty thousand words, no plot, no resolution, a man talking to himself in a basement.

It is the most modern thing written in the nineteenth century.

The Underground Man is intelligent, hypersensitive, and vicious. He is consumed by self-consciousness — aware of every motive, every contradiction, every performance. He cannot act because he is always watching himself act.

He is the first fully modern psychological subject in literature — the person who thinks too much, feels too much, and is paralyzed by the gap between interiority and action.

But he is also something more: a polemic.

The Underground Man is Dostoevsky’s attack on rationalism — on the Enlightenment belief that human beings, properly educated and rationally organized, will naturally choose what is good for them.

The Underground Man’s response:

“What is to be done with the millions of facts that bear witness that men, knowingly, that is, fully understanding their real interests, have left them in the background and rushed headlong on another path, to risk, to chance, compelled to this course by nobody and by nothing, but, as it were, precisely because they did not want the beaten track?”

Human beings, Dostoevsky insists, do not want happiness. They want freedom — even the freedom to be destructive, even the freedom to choose against their interests, even the freedom to suffer.

“What man wants is simply independent choice, whatever that independence may cost and wherever it may lead.”

This is the thesis that runs through everything Dostoevsky wrote. And it is why he saw the twentieth century coming when almost no one else did.

The Grand Inquisitor

The chapter called “The Grand Inquisitor” in The Brothers Karamazov is, in my view, the greatest single chapter in the history of the novel.

Ivan tells Alyosha a poem he has composed.

Christ returns to earth during the Spanish Inquisition. He is recognized. He begins to heal the sick. He is arrested by the Grand Inquisitor — a ninety-year-old Cardinal — and thrown into prison.

That night, the Inquisitor comes to Christ’s cell.

He speaks for pages. Christ says nothing.

The Inquisitor’s argument:

You came and offered humanity freedom. But freedom is a burden too heavy to bear. Human beings do not want freedom. They want bread, miracle, and authority — certainty, comfort, and someone to submit to.

“Instead of taking possession of men’s freedom, You increased it, and burdened the spiritual kingdom of mankind with its sufferings forever. You desired man’s free love, that he should follow Thee freely, enticed and taken captive by Thee.”

But this, the Inquisitor says, was a mistake. The Church — his Church, the church that burns heretics — has corrected your error. We have taken the freedom you gave and returned to humanity what they actually want: obedience, mystery, security.

We have made them happy. You would have made them free. We have made them happy. These are not the same thing.

The chapter ends with Christ kissing the Inquisitor’s lips. The old man releases him. Tells him never to return.

No argument. A kiss.

Dostoevsky was a deeply committed Christian. And he gave the strongest possible argument against Christianity to the villain of his greatest novel — and could only answer it with a gesture.

Because some things cannot be argued. They can only be enacted.

Crime and Punishment

Raskolnikov is a student in St. Petersburg — brilliant, poor, starving.

He has a theory: extraordinary men are above ordinary moral law. Napoleon did not hesitate. Caesar did not hesitate. He is, he believes, an extraordinary man.

He murders a pawnbroker.

The rest of the novel is his psychological disintegration.

Not because he is caught — he nearly isn’t. Not because of legal punishment — it barely registers. But because the murder itself destroys the theory that justified it.

He discovers that he is not Napoleon. Not because he lacks intelligence or will — but because the act of killing has revealed something about the nature of consciousness, guilt, and human solidarity that his theory could not account for.

You cannot step outside humanity and then step back in. The crossing is permanent.

Dostoevsky’s insight — developed forty years before Freud — is that the unconscious does not forgive what the conscious mind permits. Raskolnikov’s body rebels. His dreams accuse him. He confesses not because he is caught but because the weight of isolation becomes unbearable.

The novel is an argument that radical individualism — the Nietzschean will to power avant la lettre — is self-defeating not for moral reasons but for psychological ones.

We are social animals. We are constituted by solidarity. You cannot murder your way out of that constitution.

The Idiot

Prince Myshkin is Dostoevsky’s attempt to depict a truly good man.

He is gentle, truthful, without guile. He sees into people — their suffering, their dignity — with absolute clarity and absolute compassion.

He is also epileptic, often considered an idiot, and by the end of the novel, destroyed.

The novel’s implicit question: what happens to goodness when it encounters the world as it actually is?

The answer is not encouraging.

Myshkin does not redeem the people around him. He illuminates their suffering without being able to relieve it. He loves Nastasya Filipovna — the most damaged character in the novel — with perfect, selfless love.

She is murdered. He relapses into total idiocy.

The good man does not save anyone. He suffers with them, and is broken.

This is not nihilism. It is something harder: the insistence that goodness is real and valuable even when it fails, that the attempt to love truly is worth making even when it destroys you.

The Prophet

What makes Dostoevsky extraordinary — what separates him from every other nineteenth-century novelist — is that he saw what was coming.

In Demons (1872), written as a satire of radical nihilism, he depicted a revolutionary cell that murders one of its own members to bind the others through complicity.

He depicted the logic of totalitarianism — the use of ideology to dissolve individual conscience, the manufacture of collective guilt, the terror as a tool of cohesion — twenty years before Lenin was a political figure, seventy years before Koestler wrote Darkness at Noon.

He depicted, in the figure of Shigalyov, the revolutionary who concludes that true equality requires that nine-tenths of humanity be reduced to slavery under the direction of the enlightened one-tenth.

“I got entangled in my own data, and my conclusion directly contradicts the original idea I start from. Starting from unlimited freedom, I arrived at unlimited despotism.”

This is the twentieth century. Written in 1872.

Why He Still Matters

We live, in many ways, in the world Dostoevsky feared.

The Underground Man’s paralysis — the inability to act under the weight of self-consciousness — is the defining condition of the contemporary intellectual.

The Grand Inquisitor’s bargain — security and comfort in exchange for freedom — is the offer made by every authoritarian movement, and accepted, over and over, with relief.

Raskolnikov’s ideology — the extraordinary man above the law — reappears in every era, every culture, wearing different clothes.

And Ivan’s question — the suffering of the innocent, the impossibility of theodicy — has not been answered. It will not be answered.

Dostoevsky does not answer it either. He just refuses to look away.

That refusal — to look away from suffering, to maintain the tension between faith and despair, between freedom and the cost of freedom — is what makes him not merely a great novelist but a necessary one.

To read Dostoevsky is to be made less comfortable and more alive.

There is no higher praise I know how to give.

Start with Crime and Punishment. Then The Brothers Karamazov. Then Notes from Underground — which you will understand differently after the others. Save The Idiot for when you are ready to be broken.
entry.summary: On suffering, freedom, and the novelist who saw the twentieth century coming.

entry.title: Entropy and the Arrow of Time — Why You Cannot Unscramble an Egg

link.title: Entropy and the Arrow of Time — Why You Cannot Unscramble an Egg

entry.content:
Every fundamental law of physics is time-symmetric.

Newton’s equations: time-symmetric. Maxwell’s equations: time-symmetric. Schrödinger’s equation: time-symmetric. Even the Standard Model — with one tiny, almost negligible exception — is time-symmetric.

Run the equations forward. Run them backward. They don’t care.

And yet — you have never seen a shattered glass reassemble itself. You have never seen smoke pour back into a cigarette. You have never seen a scrambled egg unscramble.

There is a direction to time. An arrow. And only one law in physics knows about it.

The Second Law of Thermodynamics.

What Entropy Actually Is

The intuitive definition — “entropy is disorder” — is not wrong, but it is dangerously imprecise.

The correct definition is due to Boltzmann (1877):

\[S = k_B \ln \Omega\]

where \(\Omega\) is the number of microstates compatible with a given macrostate, and \(k_B\) is Boltzmann’s constant.

A macrostate is what you can observe — temperature, pressure, volume. A microstate is the exact position and momentum of every particle.

An intact egg corresponds to a small number of microstates — the proteins must be arranged just so, the yolk here, the white there. A scrambled egg corresponds to an astronomically larger number of microstates — almost any arrangement of denatured proteins looks scrambled.

The Second Law, in Boltzmann’s language:

Systems evolve toward macrostates with more microstates because there are simply more ways to be there.

This is not a force. Not a field. Not an interaction. It is pure combinatorics applied to an absurdly large number of particles.

The Boltzmann H-Theorem and Its Paradox

In 1872, Boltzmann proved the H-theorem: for a gas of particles evolving under classical mechanics, a certain quantity H decreases monotonically over time. \(-H\) is essentially entropy. So entropy increases. QED.

Except — there was a problem.

Loschmidt’s reversibility paradox (1876): If every microscopic collision is time-reversible, how can the macroscopic evolution be irreversible? For every trajectory where entropy increases, there exists a time-reversed trajectory where entropy decreases.

Boltzmann’s answer was essentially statistical: entropy-decreasing trajectories exist but are so astronomically rare that you will never observe one.

The universe, at the Big Bang, started in an extraordinarily low-entropy state. It has been evolving toward higher entropy ever since. The arrow of time is not written into the laws of physics — it is written into the initial conditions of the universe.

This is sometimes called the Past Hypothesis (David Albert, 2000): the universe began in an extremely special, low-entropy macrostate.

Why? We don’t know. This is one of the deepest open questions in physics.

Entropy and Information

In 1948, Claude Shannon — working on communication theory — defined the entropy of a probability distribution:

\[H = -\sum_i p_i \log p_i\]

This is formally identical to Boltzmann’s entropy. Not approximately. Exactly, up to constants.

This is not a coincidence.

Information and entropy are the same thing, viewed from different angles.

When you learn the microstate of a system, its entropy — from your perspective — decreases. You have gained information. The system has lost entropy.

This connection was made precise by Maxwell’s Demon (1867) — a thought experiment where a tiny demon sorts fast and slow molecules, apparently decreasing entropy without doing work.

The resolution, due to Szilard (1929) and Landauer (1961): the demon must store information about each molecule. When it erases that information — as it must, eventually — it dissipates heat into the environment.

Erasing one bit of information dissipates at least:

\[k_B T \ln 2 \text{ joules of heat}\]

This is Landauer’s principle — experimentally verified in 2012.

Information is physical. Computation has a thermodynamic cost. The demon cannot win.

Black Holes and the Entropy Bound

In 1972, Jacob Bekenstein proposed something radical:

Black holes have entropy.

Specifically, the entropy of a black hole is proportional to the area of its event horizon — not its volume:

\[S_{BH} = \frac{k_B c^3}{4 G \hbar} A = \frac{A}{4 l_P^2}\]

where \(l_P\) is the Planck length and \(A\) is the horizon area.

Hawking confirmed this by showing black holes emit thermal radiation — Hawking radiation — with a temperature:

\[T_H = \frac{\hbar c^3}{8 \pi G M k_B}\]

The Bekenstein-Hawking entropy is not a metaphor. It is the actual, physical entropy of a black hole.

This led to the holographic principle (t’Hooft, Susskind): the maximum entropy of any region of space is proportional to its boundary area, not its volume.

The universe may be, in some deep sense, a hologram — all the information needed to describe a volume of space encoded on its boundary surface.

The Heat Death of the Universe

The Second Law has a cosmological implication that is difficult to sit with.

The universe is evolving toward maximum entropy. Stars will burn out. Black holes will evaporate via Hawking radiation. Eventually — on timescales of \(10^{100}\) years and beyond — the universe will reach thermal equilibrium.

No temperature gradients. No free energy. No work possible. No life. No thought. No structure of any kind.

This is the heat death of the universe.

It is, as far as we know, inevitable.

Why This Is the Most Profound Law in Physics

The other laws of physics describe how things move. The Second Law describes why things happen at all.

Without entropy gradients — without the arrow of time — there is no causality, no memory, no narrative. You cannot remember the past because the past was lower entropy and the future is higher. The direction of time is the direction of entropy increase.

You are a low-entropy structure maintaining itself against the universal tendency toward disorder. Every thought you have, every computation your brain performs, dissipates heat into the environment — exactly as Landauer said.

Thinking has a thermodynamic cost.

The universe is running down. We are, briefly, running against it.

That, I think, is enough.
entry.summary: The only law in physics that knows which way time flows.

entry.title: Epicurus and the Art of Living Well — A Philosophy for the Present

link.title: Epicurus and the Art of Living Well — A Philosophy for the Present

entry.content:
Epicurus is the most misunderstood philosopher in the Western tradition.

His name has become an adjective — epicurean — meaning the pursuit of luxury, fine dining, sensual excess. The Oxford English Dictionary defines epicurean as “devoted to the pursuit of pleasure, especially in eating and drinking.”

This is almost the precise opposite of what Epicurus taught.

The man lived on bread, water, and occasionally cheese. He wrote: “Send me a pot of cheese, that when I like I may have a feast.”

He founded a community — The Garden — outside the walls of Athens, where he lived with friends, students, women, and slaves in deliberate simplicity, arguing that the philosophical life was the pleasurable life, and that the pleasurable life required very little.

To understand Epicurus is to understand one of the most radical and coherent answers ever given to the question: how should a human being live?

I. The Historical Epicurus

Epicurus was born in 341 BCE on the island of Samos, to Athenian parents. He began studying philosophy at fourteen. He founded his school in Athens around 307 BCE — The Garden (Κῆπος), named for the actual garden where the community met and lived.

He was extraordinarily prolific — ancient sources credit him with over 300 works. Almost nothing survives directly. We have three letters preserved by Diogenes Laërtius — the Letter to Herodotus (on physics), the Letter to Pythocles (on meteorology), and the Letter to Menoeceus (on ethics) — along with a collection of maxims, the Principal Doctrines and Vatican Sayings.

Everything else is reconstruction — from Lucretius’s magnificent poem De Rerum Natura, from Cicero’s critical accounts, from fragments quoted by later writers.

We are reading a philosopher through a keyhole. What we can see is extraordinary.

II. Atomism and the Material World

Epicurus inherited his physics from Democritus (c. 460–370 BCE), who proposed that reality consists entirely of atoms (ἄτομοι — atomos, “uncuttable”) and void.

Everything that exists — every object, every sensation, every thought, every soul — is composed of atoms moving through empty space, combining and separating according to their shapes and motions.

There is no divine providence. No cosmic plan. No teleology built into nature. The universe is matter in motion, nothing more.

Epicurus accepted this framework completely and drew its ethical consequences with extraordinary rigor.

The swerve:

Democritean atomism was deterministic — atoms moving in straight lines, their future paths determined entirely by their past collisions.

Epicurus introduced a modification: the clinamen — the swerve. Atoms, occasionally and unpredictably, deviate slightly from their paths.

This was not primarily a physical claim. It was a move to preserve free will in a material universe. If atoms moved entirely deterministically, human choice would be an illusion. The swerve broke the causal chain.

Lucretius describes it:

“When atoms move straight down through the void by their own weight, at quite uncertain times and uncertain places, they swerve a little — only enough to call it a change of direction. Without this swerve, all things would fall downward like drops of rain through the deep void — no collision, no blow would be born among the atoms — and nature would never have created anything.”

The clinamen is philosophically awkward — an ad hoc addition to an otherwise elegant system. But the problem it addresses — determinism versus free will — remains unsolved two thousand years later.

III. The Ethics of Pleasure

Epicurus was a hedonist — from hēdonē (ἡδονή), pleasure.

But his hedonism was nothing like its popular caricature.

He distinguished between two types of pleasure:

Kinetic pleasures (ἡδοναὶ ἐν κινήσει) — pleasures involving active enjoyment: eating, drinking, sex, entertainment. These are real pleasures. Epicurus did not deny them. But they are unstable — they require constant renewal, they produce desire when absent, and excessive pursuit of them generates anxiety and disturbance.
