Katastematic pleasures (καταστηματικαὶ ἡδοναί) — pleasures of stable, undisturbed states: the absence of pain in the body (aponia), the absence of disturbance in the soul (ataraxia).

The highest pleasure, for Epicurus, is ataraxia — a Greek word meaning literally “not disturbed,” a state of tranquil equilibrium, freedom from anxiety, fear, and mental turbulence.

This is a counterintuitive claim that deserves to be taken seriously.

Epicurus is saying: the greatest pleasure is not the peak experience — the finest wine, the most intense sexual encounter, the most exciting adventure. The greatest pleasure is the stable background condition in which pain and anxiety are absent.

Compare this to contemporary hedonic psychology.

Hedonic adaptation: humans rapidly adapt to positive changes in their circumstances. The new house, the promotion, the relationship — the pleasure diminishes as you habituate to it. You return to your hedonic baseline.

The peak-end rule (Kahneman): we evaluate experiences by their peak and their ending, not their duration. A longer pleasant experience is not rated as more pleasant than a shorter one of similar peak intensity.

Wanting versus liking (Berridge): the dopaminergic wanting system and the opioid liking system are separable. You can intensely want something you don’t like. Desire and satisfaction are neurologically distinct.

Epicurus, without neuroscience, arrived at the same insight: the pursuit of intense pleasures generates desire that is itself painful — a craving that cannot be satisfied because satisfaction only generates new craving.

The solution is not asceticism — not the denial of pleasure. It is the cultivation of pleasures that do not generate craving: simple food enjoyed mindfully, friendship, philosophical conversation, the contemplation of nature.

“It is not the young man who should be considered fortunate, but the old man who has lived well; because the young man in his prime wanders much by chance, vacillating in his beliefs, while the old man has come to anchor in old age as though in port.”

IV. The Tetrapharmakos — The Four-Fold Cure

The Epicurean school summarized its philosophy in a formula known as the Tetrapharmakos (τετραφάρμακος) — the four-fold remedy, the four medicines for the soul:

Ἄφοβον ὁ θεός, ἀνύποπτον ὁ θάνατος, καὶ τἀγαθὸν μὲν εὔκτητον, τὸ δὲ δεινὸν εὐεκκαρτέρητον.

Do not fear god. Do not fear death. What is good is easy to get. What is terrible is easy to endure.

Each line is a philosophical argument compressed to an aphorism.

Do not fear god.

The Epicurean gods exist — Epicurus was not an atheist — but they are perfectly blessed beings, living in the spaces between worlds, entirely indifferent to human affairs.

They did not create the universe. They do not intervene in it. They do not reward or punish. They have no interest in us whatsoever.

Therefore: there is nothing to fear from them. No divine punishment. No cosmic judgment. No need for sacrifice, prayer, or propitiation.

This was not a comfortable position in ancient Athens. Epicurus was accused of atheism throughout antiquity — the accusation was inaccurate but understandable.

Do not fear death.

This is Epicurus’s most famous argument, and one of the most powerful in the history of philosophy.

“Death is nothing to us; for that which is dissolved is without sensation, and that which lacks sensation is nothing to us.”

The argument: death is the cessation of consciousness. Where death is, you are not. Where you are, death is not. You will never experience death — only the approach of it.

The pre-birth argument, his most penetrating:

You did not exist before you were born. Were you harmed by your non-existence before birth? Was the infinite time before your birth a deprivation?

If not — why should the infinite time after your death be any different?

The asymmetry we feel — dreading future non-existence but not minding past non-existence — is, Epicurus argues, irrational. The states are symmetrical.

Thomas Nagel’s famous paper “Death” (1970) challenges this argument: death is a harm because it deprives us of future goods. Pre-birth non-existence deprives us of past goods, but goods can only be goods for a subject, and the subject didn’t exist yet to be deprived.

The debate continues. But Epicurus’s argument retains its force — not perhaps as a logical proof, but as a therapeutic reframing.

If the fear of death is a fear of suffering — you will not suffer. You will not be there to suffer. If it is a fear of missing out — you will not be there to miss anything. The subject who would experience the loss does not exist.

“Why should I fear death? If I am, death is not. If death is, I am not.”

What is good is easy to get.

The natural and necessary desires — food, water, shelter, companionship, philosophical conversation — are easily satisfied. They do not require wealth, status, or power.

The desires that are hard to satisfy — luxury, fame, dominance, endless novelty — are not natural. They are culturally constructed, philosophically confused, and reliably productive of misery.

“To whom little is not enough, nothing is enough.”

This is a radical political claim disguised as personal ethics. The consumer economy depends on the proposition that desire is infinite and satisfaction is always temporary. Epicurus said: you have been deceived about what you need.

What is terrible is easy to endure.

Either pain is severe and brief, or it is chronic and mild. Extreme suffering does not last — the body gives out. Chronic pain allows the mind to find compensations, to retreat into philosophical reflection, to maintain ataraxia even under difficult conditions.

Epicurus wrote this from personal experience. He suffered from chronic kidney stones and stomach ailments for most of his adult life. His last letter, written on the day of his death, describes intense physical pain — and also describes it as outweighed by the joy of philosophical conversation with a friend.

Whether you find this heroic or implausible probably depends on your own relationship with pain.

V. Friendship

For Epicurus, friendship was not peripheral to the good life. It was central to it.

“Of all the things which wisdom provides to make us entirely happy, much the greatest is the possession of friendship.”

This is not sentimental. It is a philosophical claim about the structure of human flourishing.

The Epicurean community — The Garden — was built around friendship. Men and women, free and enslaved, ate together, argued together, consoled each other, studied together. This was radical in ancient Athens.

Why is friendship so central?

Because ataraxia — the tranquil undisturbedness of the soul — cannot be achieved alone.

We are social animals. We need to be known. We need the security that comes from being cared for and caring for others. The solitary philosopher pursuing pleasure in isolation will find himself haunted by anxieties that friendship would have dissolved.

But Epicurean friendship is also instrumentally rational — and Epicurus was honest about this in a way that makes modern readers uncomfortable.

He said: we value friends partly for the help they can provide. We maintain friendships partly because of what we get from them.

This seems cynical. But Epicurus’s point is more subtle: there is no sharp line between self-interest and altruism in a well-functioning friendship. Your friend’s wellbeing is your wellbeing. Their joy is your joy. Self-interest, properly understood, includes the interests of those you genuinely love.

“It is not so much our friends’ help that helps us as the confidence of their help.”

VI. The Examined Life in The Garden

What did a day in the Epicurean Garden look like?

Simple food. Philosophical conversation. Reading and writing. Walking in the garden. Occasional wine — never to excess. Friendship, sustained over years.

Epicurus had a practice that anticipates modern cognitive behavioral therapy:

Every evening, he recommended reviewing the day’s actions and asking three questions: What did I do wrong? What did I do right? What could I have done better?

Not for self-punishment — Epicurus was opposed to guilt as a source of suffering. But for progress — the slow, deliberate improvement of one’s character through honest self-examination.

This is the philosophical life as practice, not theory — philosophy not as an academic subject but as a daily discipline for living well.

Seneca, the Stoic who read Epicurus carefully despite the rivalry between the schools, put it this way:

“Philosophy promises above all: common sense, humanity, and fellowship.”

VII. Epicurus Against Epicureanism

The irony of Epicurus’s legacy is total.

A man who lived on bread and water, who valued friendship above pleasure, who taught that the fear of death was irrational and that simple pleasures were the highest — gave his name to a philosophy of luxury and excess.

The misreading has ancient roots. Cicero, who opposed Epicureanism for political reasons, caricatured it as hedonism. The Stoics, the Epicureans’ great rivals, attacked them as pleasure-seekers who lacked moral seriousness.

The caricature stuck.

But read the texts.

“We must exercise ourselves in the things which bring happiness, since, if that be present, we have everything, and, if that be absent, all our actions are directed toward attaining it.”

“The misfortune of the wise is better than the prosperity of the fool.”

“Do not spoil what you have by desiring what you have not; remember that what you now have was once among the things you only hoped for.”

This is not a philosophy of indulgence. It is a philosophy of attention — to what you actually have, to what you actually need, to the people in front of you, to the present moment that is slipping away while you pursue the future one.

VIII. What Epicurus Gets Right

The 21st century has produced a vast literature on happiness, wellbeing, flourishing.

Positive psychology. Mindfulness. Hedonic adaptation. The science of happiness. Flow states. Gratitude practice.

Almost all of it was anticipated by Epicurus.

The research on hedonic adaptation confirms his point about kinetic pleasures: we habituate, we return to baseline, the pursuit of intense pleasures does not produce lasting happiness.

The research on social connection confirms his point about friendship: relationship quality is the single strongest predictor of long-term wellbeing in the Harvard Study of Adult Development, running for over 80 years.

The research on negative visualization — the practice of imagining the loss of what you have — as a tool for appreciating the present is explicit in Epicurus: “Think how happy you are with what you have, by imagining how much you would miss it if you didn’t have it.”

The mindfulness movement — the cultivation of present-moment attention, the reduction of ruminative anxiety — is Epicurean in its structure, even when it presents itself as Buddhist.

Epicurus diagnosed the human condition with extraordinary precision: we suffer not because our circumstances are bad but because our desires are miscalibrated, our fears are irrational, and we are incapable of inhabiting the present.

The cure is philosophical — but philosophy as practice, as daily discipline, as the slow retraining of attention.

IX. The Limits of Epicureanism

Epicurus is not the last word.

His ethics is essentially private — it says almost nothing about political life, about justice, about our obligations to those outside our circle of friends.

His advice — withdraw from political life, cultivate your garden, find peace in friendship and simple pleasures — was given in a specific historical moment: the collapse of the Greek polis, the rise of Macedonian power, the sense that the individual could no longer meaningfully affect the political world.

In a different historical moment — our moment, perhaps — this counsel feels insufficient.

The person who achieves perfect ataraxia while their society deteriorates around them has achieved something — but not everything.

There are also questions Epicurus cannot answer.

His treatment of justice is thin — justice is essentially a contract, a mutual agreement not to harm each other, grounded in self-interest. This cannot account for our obligations to those who cannot reciprocate — future generations, animals, the severely disabled.

His treatment of meaning — of the sense that a life should amount to something, that the projects we devote ourselves to matter — is underdeveloped. Ataraxia is a negative ideal: the absence of disturbance. Many people want something more — a sense of purpose, of contribution, of significance.

Epicurus would say: that want is itself a source of anxiety, and perhaps you would be happier without it.

He might be right. Many people would not find that satisfying.

Coda

Epicurus died in 270 BCE, from kidney stones — painful, prolonged.

His last letter, to his friend Idomeneus:

“On this blissful day, which is also the last of my life, I write this to you. My continual sufferings from strangury and dysentery are so great that nothing could augment them; but I set over against them all the joy in my heart at the memory of my past conversations with you.”

He died as he had lived — finding in friendship and in philosophical memory a joy sufficient to outweigh physical pain.

Whether you believe that or not, you must admit it is a remarkable way to end.

Not with fear. Not with regret. With gratitude, for the conversations.

Further reading: Epicurus — Letter to Menoeceus (trans. Brad Inwood & L.P. Gerson) Lucretius — De Rerum Natura (trans. A.E. Stallings — the best translation) Catherine Wilson — Epicureanism at the Origins of Modernity (2008) Phillip Mitsis — Epicurus’ Ethical Theory (1988) Tim O’Keefe — Epicureanism (2010)
entry.summary: On pleasure, death, friendship, and why the most misunderstood philosopher in history was probably right.

entry.title: Gogol’s Overcoat — On Invisibility, Desire, and the Weight of a Name

link.title: Gogol’s Overcoat — On Invisibility, Desire, and the Weight of a Name

entry.content:
“We all came out from under Gogol’s overcoat.”

This line is attributed to Dostoevsky. Scholars dispute whether he said it. It doesn’t matter.

It is true.

Every Russian writer who came after Gogol — Dostoevsky, Turgenev, Tolstoy, Chekhov — emerged from the literary universe that The Overcoat (1842) created: the universe of the small man, the bureaucratic labyrinth, the city as indifferent monster, the soul crushed by paperwork and poverty until it becomes almost nothing — and then, in the moment of becoming nothing, becomes everything.

I. Gogol Himself

Before the story, the man.

Nikolai Vasilyevich Gogol was born in 1809 in Sorochyntsi, in what is now Ukraine. He died in 1852 in Moscow, having burned the second volume of Dead Souls — his masterwork, years in the making — nine days before his death.

He was forty-two years old.

In the last years of his life, Gogol fell under the influence of a fanatical priest, Father Matvei Konstantinovsky, who convinced him that his literary work was sinful, that laughter was the devil’s instrument, that he must mortify the flesh.

Gogol stopped eating. He burned his manuscript. He turned his face to the wall.

He had spent his career laughing at the world — a laughter that was never entirely comfortable, that always had something desperate in it, something that was not quite laughter.

In the end, the laugh stopped.

What remained: the work. And the work is unlike anything else in literature.

II. The Name

The Overcoat begins with a name.

“In the department of… but it is better not to mention the department. There is nothing more irritable than departments, regiments, bureaus — in a word, any kind of official body. Nowadays every individual considers the whole of society insulted in his person…”

Gogol refuses to name the department. He will name almost nothing precisely — the city is suggested but never confirmed as St. Petersburg, the department is gestures-at but unnamed.

But the protagonist is named. In extraordinary detail.

His name is Akaki Akakiyeviç Başmaçkin.

Akaki — from the Greek ákakos, meaning innocent, guileless, without malice. Twice. Akaki Akakiyeviç — son of Akaki. The innocent son of the innocent.

And Başmaçkin — from the Russian başmak, meaning shoe. The man whose family name derives from footwear.

The name is the first joke. And the first cruelty.

Gogol spends two paragraphs on how this name came to be — the mother’s difficulty choosing, the rejection of other names (Mokkiya, Sosiya, Khozdazat, Trifiliy, Dula, Varakhasiy), the resigned acceptance of the father’s name because there was simply no better option.

“So that is how he came to be called Akaki Akakiyeviç.”

A man who inherits his father’s name because nothing better was available. A man whose family name means shoe. A man who will spend the entire story consumed by a desire for a coat.

Footwear. Overcoat. The man is made of clothing — and nothing else, the story will suggest, until the coat makes him briefly otherwise.

III. The Invisible Man

Akaki Akakiyeviç is a copying clerk in a government department in St. Petersburg.

His job: to copy documents. Not to compose them. Not to summarize them. To copy. He copies with a devotion that goes beyond duty — beyond even enthusiasm — into something that can only be called love.

“It would be hard to find another man who lived so entirely for his work. It was not enough to say that he worked with zeal; no, he worked with love. In his copying he found a varied and agreeable world of his own.”

He has no friends. No family. No social life. When colleagues invite him somewhere, he declines — he has copying to do at home. He carries his copying home in his head, smiling at particular letters as he walks.

He is, at first glance, a comic figure. A man so reduced that his interior life consists entirely of the pleasure of forming letters correctly.

But Gogol will not let us laugh entirely.

The colleagues tease him. They invent humiliations — drop paper on his head, tell him he is to be married, breathe smoke in his face. He does not react. He does not complain.

“Only when the joke became too unbearable — when someone jogged his elbow and prevented him from doing his work — did he say: ‘Leave me alone. Why do you torment me?’ And in those words and in the voice in which they were uttered there was something strange — something that moved to pity.”

A young man, newly arrived at the department, who had begun tormenting Akaki with the others — stops. Suddenly.

“And long after, in moments of great merriment, the figure of the little clerk with the bald patch on his head would appear before him with the words: ‘Leave me alone. Why do you torment me?’ And in these words he could hear the sound of others: ‘I am your brother.’”

I am your brother.

These three words — appearing without warning in the middle of what seemed like a comic story — are the pivot on which the entire story turns.

Gogol has been making us laugh. Now he makes us ashamed of laughing.

IV. The Coat

Akaki Akakiyeviç’s coat is falling apart.

The tailor Petrovich — a one-eyed, pockmarked man who charges more when sober and can be negotiated with when drunk — tells him: it cannot be repaired. He needs a new coat.

The cost: one hundred and fifty rubles. More than Akaki earns in several months.

And here the story transforms.

The coat becomes the object of desire — the single, consuming goal that gives Akaki’s life a shape it never had before.

He saves. He stops drinking tea in the evening. He wears his old coat in the house to preserve it. He walks on tiptoe across cobblestones to save his soles. He stops having his laundry done — instead he takes it off at home and wears only his dressing gown.

“At first it was somewhat difficult for him to accustom himself to these privations, but after a while it became a habit, and went smoothly enough; he even became quite accustomed to being hungry in the evening; on the other hand, he had spiritual sustenance, for he carried ever in his thoughts the idea of his future coat.”

The coat is his telos — his end, his purpose. The coat gives Akaki Akakiyeviç, for the first time in the story, a future.

A man with no desires has no future. A man with a desire — even this desire, even a coat — has something to live toward.

This is Gogol’s first philosophical move: desire, even trivial desire, is what constitutes temporality. Without wanting, there is only the eternal present of copying.

The coat, when it finally arrives, is described with extraordinary tenderness:

“The coat was carefully examined and found to be in perfect order. Then they brought it out and put it on him… Akaki Akakiyeviç was a connoisseur of fine cloth, and he could not help admiring its quality…”

He walks through the city in his new coat. He is noticed — for the first time in his life, noticed. Colleagues congratulate him. He is invited to a party.

For one evening, Akaki Akakiyeviç exists socially. He has, through a garment, been admitted to the human community.

V. The Theft

Walking home from the party, at the edge of a deserted square, two men stop him.

They take the coat.

The scene is described with almost no detail — a few lines. Abrupt, brutal, over.

The coat is gone.

Akaki reports the theft. He is sent to the police, then to a “Person of Consequence” — a newly promoted official who has adopted the manner of importance through shouting, slowness, and administrative brutality.

The Person of Consequence shouts at Akaki for going through improper channels.

“Do you know to whom you are speaking? Do you understand who stands before you? Do you understand? Do you understand? I ask you!”

Akaki, trembling, leaves. He does not know how he gets home.

He takes to his bed. He develops a fever. He dies.

“And St. Petersburg was left without Akaki Akakiyeviç, as though he had never been in it at all.”

VI. The Philosophical Weight of Disappearance

That sentence — as though he had never been in it at all — is the true center of the story.

Gogol has spent the entire narrative establishing the precise texture of this man’s invisibility. He was always already not there. The coat — the desire for the coat, the having of the coat — briefly made him present. Its removal returns him to absence.

And the city does not notice.

This is not tragic in the traditional sense — Akaki is not a great man brought low by fate. He is a small man who was always low, briefly lifted, and then restored to his original position.

The tragedy is not his fall. It is that there was nothing to fall from.

Hegel wrote about the unhappy consciousness — the awareness of a gap between the self as it is and the self as it ought to be, a gap that cannot be closed.

Akaki has no unhappy consciousness. He is, until the coat, incapable of the awareness that would constitute unhappiness in the Hegelian sense.

The coat gives him this awareness. It shows him what it is to be seen, to be present, to exist socially. Its theft takes this awareness away but leaves behind the knowledge that it existed.

That is what kills him. Not the cold. Not the fever. The knowledge of what he had, briefly, been.

VII. The Ghost

But the story does not end with Akaki’s death.

After his death, a ghost begins to appear in St. Petersburg — near the Kalinkin Bridge — tearing overcoats from passersby.

The ghost is Akaki Akakiyeviç.

“The ghost of the clerk appeared, looking for the stolen overcoat, and under the pretext of the stolen overcoat tearing all sorts of overcoats off the shoulders of all sorts of people.”

The ghost eventually finds the Person of Consequence — who has by now felt some remorse for his treatment of Akaki — and tears the coat from his shoulders.

“The ghost of the clerk appeared to him and said: ‘Ah, so here you are at last! At last I’ve got you by the collar! Your coat is the very one I need! You refused to help me, and have abused me into the bargain! So now give up your coat!’”

The Person of Consequence is terrified. He stops shouting at subordinates. He treats people better.

The ghost, having achieved something, disappears — though rumors persist of it wandering the outskirts of the city.

VIII. What the Ghost Means

The supernatural ending of The Overcoat has been interpreted in two incompatible ways — and the incompatibility is, I think, deliberate.

The social reading: The ghost is justice — or the demand for justice. Akaki in life was powerless, invisible, dismissed. In death he can do what he could not do alive: take back, demand, terrify the powerful. The supernatural gives him the power that the social order denied him.

This reading produces a politically satisfying story — the oppressed, in death if not in life, achieves redress.

The existential reading: The ghost cannot stop. He takes the coat from the Person of Consequence — but this does not bring him peace. He is still seen, still wandering, still tearing coats.

The desire survives death. The wound does not heal by being inflicted on another.

What Akaki could not have in life — recognition, presence, the warmth of being seen — cannot be obtained by haunting. The ghost is not satisfied. The ghost is still wanting.

Gogol does not resolve this. The story ends ambiguously — the ghost still out there, somewhere, still wanting its coat.

IX. Desire, Recognition, and Hegel Again

The deepest philosophical reading of The Overcoat connects to Hegel’s concept of recognition (Anerkennung).

For Hegel, the self is not constituted in isolation — it requires recognition by another self to be fully real. The famous master-slave dialectic: the master is recognized by the slave, but the slave’s recognition is not truly satisfying because the slave is not an equal. Full recognition — genuine selfhood — requires mutual recognition between equals.

Akaki has no recognition. He is seen but not recognized — noticed as an object of mockery, not acknowledged as a subject.

The coat, temporarily, gives him recognition. Colleagues notice him. They smile. They invite him. For one evening, he is the recipient of genuine social acknowledgment.

The theft takes not just the coat but this recognition. And recognition, once felt and then withdrawn, is more painful than its absence.

This is the psychological precision at the center of the story: Gogol understands that deprivation is not simply the absence of something — it is the knowledge of the absence. Akaki before the coat did not suffer his invisibility because he could not conceive of visibility. Akaki after the coat — even for that one evening — has the concept, and therefore the lack.

The overcoat is not a coat. It is consciousness.

X. Gogol’s Laughter

The Overcoat is classified as a comic story.

It begins with jokes. The name is a joke. Petrovich the tailor is a joke. The bureaucratic machinery is a joke.

But the laughter progressively thickens — becomes uncomfortable, becomes uneasy, becomes finally impossible to sustain.

“I am your brother.”

That moment — that sudden apparition of genuine pathos in the middle of a comic scene — is the signature of Gogol’s method.

He is often called the father of Russian realism. He is nothing of the kind. His world is not realistic — it is grotesque, hallucinatory, operating by a logic that is adjacent to realism but fundamentally distorted.

The Nose falls off a man’s face and starts walking around St. Petersburg. Dead souls are purchased as an economic asset. A coat haunts the people who failed to protect it.

Gogol’s grotesque is not surrealism — it is not arbitrary or decorative. It is a way of making visible what realism cannot show: the inner logic of a society so distorted that realism, applied straightforwardly, would produce something that looked like satire.

The only way to show the truth about this world is to make the world even more itself — to take the distortion and amplify it until it becomes visible as distortion.

The coat that becomes a man’s entire soul. The theft of the coat that becomes the theft of a soul. The ghost that haunts because the social order would not give what it owed.

None of this is realistic. All of it is true.

XI. Akaki Akakiyeviç and Us

Why does this story still matter?

Because Akaki Akakiyeviç is not a historical curiosity — a portrait of 19th century Russian bureaucracy — but a recognizable human type that the 21st century produces in extraordinary quantities.

The person whose interiority has been hollowed out by repetitive, meaningless work — who finds in some small, specific object or activity the concentrated meaning that their labor denies them.

The person who is invisible in the social world — who passes through institutions without being seen, who has no advocate, no protector, no one who would notice if they were gone.

The person whose desire for recognition — for the simple acknowledgment that they exist, that they matter, that they are a subject and not merely an object — is met with indifference, bureaucratic obstruction, and finally shouting.

“Do you know to whom you are speaking?”

We know this voice. It speaks in every institution, in every encounter between the powerful and the powerless, in every interaction where someone with status uses that status not to help but to display.

And we know this too: the moment of recognition — however brief — and what it costs to have it taken away.

Gogol wrote this story in 1842. It is set in St. Petersburg. It is about right now. It is about here. It is about the person in the cubicle and the person in the waiting room and the person who has been waiting for an answer for weeks, from an institution that has forgotten they exist.

I am your brother.

Coda: The Coat and the Name

At the end, let us return to the beginning.

The story opens with a name — Akaki Akakiyeviç Başmaçkin — given reluctantly, by default, because nothing better was available.

A man named after footwear, who desires a coat, who briefly has it, who loses it, who dies, who haunts.

The coat and the name are the same thing. Both are coverings. Both are the social self — the self that is visible to others, the self that exists in the social world, the self that can be seen and recognized.

Without the coat, Akaki is just the name — just the formal, bureaucratic designation, the mark in the register, the unit of copying labor.

With the coat, he is briefly a person.

Gogol’s story is about what it costs to be a person — how much of what we are is constituted by how we appear, by how we are seen, by whether someone has looked at us and seen not just a function but a face.

Akaki wanted a coat. What he wanted was to exist.

He existed, briefly. That was enough to destroy him.

That is the story. That is Gogol.

Further reading: Nikolai Gogol — The Overcoat and Other Stories (trans. Richard Pevear & Larissa Volokhonsky) Vladimir Nabokov — Nikolai Gogol (1944) — the best book ever written about Gogol Dostoevsky — Poor Folk (1846) — the direct response to The Overcoat Walter Benjamin — The Storyteller (1936) Simone Weil — Affliction and the Love of God (1942) — on invisibility and suffering
entry.summary: A close reading of the strangest, most devastating short story ever written — and what it says about being human.

entry.title: Kant — The Man Who Never Left Königsberg and Changed Everything

link.title: Kant — The Man Who Never Left Königsberg and Changed Everything

entry.content:
Immanuel Kant never traveled more than ten miles from his birthplace.

He was born in Königsberg, Prussia, in 1724. He studied in Königsberg, taught in Königsberg, wrote in Königsberg, and died in Königsberg in 1804.

He never married. He had no children. His daily routine was so precise that the citizens of Königsberg reportedly set their clocks by his afternoon walk — every day, at exactly 3:30 PM, Kant would leave his house and walk the same route.

There is a story — probably apocryphal, but too good not to tell — that Kant missed his walk on only two occasions in his adult life.

The first: the day he received Rousseau’s Émile. He was so absorbed he forgot to walk.

The second: the day news arrived of the French Revolution. He was so excited he forgot to walk.

Rousseau and revolution — the two things that could stop Kant’s clock.

This tells you almost everything you need to know about Immanuel Kant.

I. The Man

Let us dwell for a moment on Kant the person, because he is one of the most entertaining figures in the history of philosophy — not because he was dramatic or scandalous, but because he was so magnificently, catastrophically boring in his personal life and so completely, utterly revolutionary in his thought.

He was small — around five feet tall. He had a slightly deformed chest, which he compensated for by an extraordinarily rigid posture and precise dress. He was obsessed with his health, developing elaborate theories about digestion, breathing, and sleep that he applied to himself with scientific rigor.

He had a theory that you should breathe only through your nose — mouth breathing was dangerous — and he reportedly would cross the street to avoid walking behind someone who might breathe on him.

He dined with friends every day — always exactly at noon, always for exactly four hours — but had a rule that the number of guests should be at least as many as the Graces (three) and no more than the Muses (nine).

He was a brilliant conversationalist on every topic except philosophy. At dinner, he refused to discuss philosophy. He talked about science, politics, literature, travel — he was particularly interested in geography and travel writing, having never traveled anywhere himself.

His lectures were famous — witty, accessible, filled with examples. Students traveled from across Europe to attend them. His published prose was the opposite: dense, technical, relentlessly systematic, requiring multiple readings to yield meaning.

He was aware of this. He reportedly said that his Critique of Pure Reason — the most important work of philosophy written in the last 300 years — was unreadable.

He was right. It is.

Let us read it anyway.

II. The Problem Kant Was Trying to Solve

To understand what Kant did, you need to understand what was broken before him.

By the mid-18th century, European philosophy had split into two camps that could not talk to each other.

The Rationalists — Descartes, Spinoza, Leibniz — argued that the most important truths could be known through reason alone, independent of experience. Mathematics was the model: 2 + 2 = 4 is true regardless of what you observe. The rationalists believed that God, the soul, causation — the deepest features of reality — could be demonstrated through pure reasoning.

The Empiricists — Locke, Berkeley, Hume — argued that all knowledge comes from experience. The mind at birth is a blank slate — tabula rasa. Everything we know, we learned from our senses. There are no innate ideas.

Hume took empiricism to its logical conclusion, and the conclusion was devastating.

If all knowledge comes from experience — then causation cannot be known.

You observe the billiard ball strike another and the other move. You observe this repeatedly. But you never observe the necessity of the connection — the fact that the first ball must cause the second to move. You only observe constant conjunction. The idea of necessary causal connection is not in the data — it is something the mind adds.

And if causation is just a habit of the mind — then science, which is built on causal laws, has no rational foundation.

Hume, by his own account, was left in skeptical despair.

Kant read Hume and — famously — said that Hume “awoke him from his dogmatic slumber.”

The Critique of Pure Reason (1781) is his response.

It took him eleven years to think about it. He wrote it in four to five months. The haste shows. It is one of the most disorganized great books ever written. It is also one of the most important.

III. The Copernican Revolution in Philosophy

Kant’s move was breathtaking in its audacity.

The traditional assumption: the mind conforms to objects. We perceive reality as it is because our minds accurately represent the world. Knowledge is the mind’s attempt to match reality.

Kant’s reversal: objects conform to the mind.

Not because the mind creates reality — Kant was not an idealist in that sense. But because the mind imposes a structure on experience through which all objects must be filtered before they can appear to us at all.

He called this the Copernican Revolution in philosophy.

Copernicus stopped asking why the stars move across the sky and instead asked: what if the observer is moving? The question is the same; the perspective is reversed.

Kant stopped asking how the mind comes to know reality and instead asked: what structures must the mind possess for experience to be possible at all?

The answer: space, time, and the categories of the understanding.

IV. Space and Time Are Not Out There

This is where Kant gets genuinely strange.

Space and time, for Kant, are not features of reality as it is in itself. They are the forms of intuition — the structures through which the mind organizes experience.

We do not perceive objects in space and time because space and time are properties of objects. We perceive objects in space and time because space and time are the lens through which the mind processes all sensory input.

You cannot experience anything that is not spatial and temporal because your mind cannot process anything that has not been run through the spatial-temporal filter.

This means: you will never know what reality is like independent of the structures your mind imposes on it.

The thing-in-itself — das Ding an sich — is forever inaccessible. You only ever know the phenomenon — reality as it appears through the structures of your cognition.

This is not skepticism — Kant is not saying we know nothing. He is saying: we know the phenomenal world with certainty, including scientific knowledge. But the ultimate nature of reality — noumenal reality — is beyond our grasp.

Kant’s position: we have reliable knowledge of appearances. We have no knowledge of things as they are in themselves.

The immediate implication: all the traditional metaphysics — proofs of God’s existence, the immortality of the soul, the freedom of the will — go beyond possible experience and therefore cannot be established by theoretical reason.

Kant spent a considerable portion of the Critique systematically dismantling every metaphysical argument for God, soul, and freedom.

Then, in the Critique of Practical Reason, he argued that morality requires us to postulate them anyway.

He is the most systematic philosopher in history. He is also, in a very specific sense, a troll.

V. Synthetic A Priori Judgments

Kant introduced a distinction that has been fought over ever since.

Analytic judgments: true by definition. “All bachelors are unmarried.” The predicate is contained in the subject. These are necessarily true but tell us nothing new.

Synthetic judgments: tell us something new. “The cat is on the mat.” The predicate is not contained in the subject. These are informative but contingent.

A priori: known independently of experience. A posteriori: known through experience.

Hume assumed: analytic = a priori, synthetic = a posteriori. You either know by definition or by observation. Those are the only options.

Kant’s bombshell: there is a third category. Synthetic a priori judgments: necessarily true, known independently of experience, AND genuinely informative about the world.

His examples:

  • “7 + 5 = 12” — not true by definition (you must actually add), but necessarily true and known a priori.
  • “Every event has a cause” — not true by definition, but necessarily true and not derived from experience.
  • Geometry: “The straight line between two points is the shortest” — not contained in the concept of a straight line, but necessarily and a priori true.

These are possible, Kant argues, because they describe not reality as it is in itself but the structure the mind imposes on experience. We know that every event has a cause a priori because causation is one of the categories of the understanding — a structural feature of how the mind processes experience.

Without the category of causation, we couldn’t have coherent experience at all.

This is elegant. It is also, as Hume’s defenders point out, somewhat circular. We know causation applies to experience because causation is part of what makes experience possible. But why should we trust that the categories of our cognition track anything real?

Kant’s answer: because they are the conditions of possible experience. If they weren’t reliable for the phenomenal world, we wouldn’t have coherent experience to philosophize about.

VI. The Categorical Imperative

Kant’s ethics is, by his own standards, derived entirely from reason.

He was contemptuous of moral theories grounded in feeling, sympathy, consequences, or happiness. Not because these things don’t matter — but because they cannot ground obligation.

I might feel sympathy for you today and not tomorrow. My feelings are unreliable. If morality is grounded in feelings, morality is unreliable. But moral obligation is necessarily binding — it does not vary. Therefore, morality must be grounded in reason.

The Categorical Imperative — his supreme principle of morality — has several formulations. The most famous:

Formula of Universal Law:

“Act only according to that maxim whereby you can at the same time will that it should become a universal law.”

In other words: before acting, ask — what if everyone did this?

If you are considering lying to get out of trouble, ask: what if everyone lied whenever convenient? The institution of truthful communication would collapse. Your lie depends on others telling the truth. You cannot universalize your maxim without contradiction. Therefore: do not lie.

This sounds like the Golden Rule. It is not quite the same. The Golden Rule is about reciprocity — treat others as you wish to be treated. The Categorical Imperative is about universalizability — could the principle of your action be a universal law?

Formula of Humanity:

“Act so that you treat humanity, whether in your own person or in that of another, always as an end and never as a means only.”

This is arguably the most influential single sentence in the history of ethics.

Never use a person merely as a tool for your purposes. Every human being has a dignity — a worth that is not conditional on usefulness — that must be respected absolutely.

This grounds: the prohibition on slavery, the prohibition on torture, the prohibition on using people as experimental subjects without consent, the prohibition on treating people as statistics.

The Nuremberg Code, the Declaration of Helsinki, the entire framework of research ethics — these are Kantian in their structure, even when their authors didn’t know it.

VII. The Autonomy of the Will

Kant’s deepest ethical insight is also his most demanding.

Moral worth, for Kant, comes not from consequences, not from following God’s commands, not from social convention — but from autonomy: giving the law to yourself through reason.

Auto-nomos — self-legislating.

A person who acts morally because they fear punishment is not acting morally — they are acting prudently. A person who acts morally because they feel sympathy is not acting morally — they are acting emotionally. A person who acts morally because their culture told them to is not acting morally — they are acting conventionally.

Moral action requires: acting from the recognition that reason demands this — and choosing it for that reason alone.

This is extraordinarily demanding. It implies that most of what we call moral behavior — driven by habit, feeling, social pressure, self-interest — lacks genuine moral worth.

It also implies something Kant found important: the dignity of moral agency.

Because you are capable of autonomous moral action — because you can give the law to yourself through reason — you have a dignity that is absolutely unconditional. Not because you are useful. Not because you are loved. Because you are a rational agent, capable of autonomy.

This is the philosophical foundation of human rights.

VIII. The Things Kant Got Wrong

In the spirit of honest philosophical engagement:

The universalizability test is underspecified.

Almost any maxim can be universalized with the right description. “Lie to murderers asking where your friends are hiding” — can this be universalized? Kant said no. Most people disagree.

The problem: the test depends heavily on how you describe the maxim. Kant’s framework does not tell you how to describe it.

The absolute prohibition on lying.

Kant famously argued that lying is always wrong — even to a murderer asking where your friend is hiding.

This conclusion is so obviously wrong that generations of philosophers have proposed rescues — finding ways to interpret Kant that avoid it. Kant himself refused rescue. He meant it.

The friend who knocks on your door and asks you to hide them — you lie to the murderer — and Kant says you have acted wrongly.

This is a case where the theory has clearly run ahead of the morality.

The treatment of women, non-Europeans, and animals.

Kant’s ethics grants full moral status only to rational agents. He defined rationality in ways that excluded women from full moral and political standing — a conclusion he drew explicitly and without embarrassment.

His writings on non-European peoples are worse. By his own ethical standards — treating persons as ends — his actual views about non-European peoples are not merely wrong but incoherent.

The universalist ethics and the parochial prejudices coexist in his work in a way that should make us deeply suspicious of philosophers who claim their personal limitations don’t affect their universal theories.

IX. What Kant Got Permanently Right

Despite everything —

The critical turn: the insight that we cannot ask what reality is like without first asking what kind of knowers we are. This is still the right starting point for epistemology.

The dignity of persons: the idea that rational agents have a worth that is not conditional on usefulness or results. This is the philosophical bedrock of human rights. Whatever its origins in Kant’s specific framework, the insight survives the framework.

The autonomy of morality: the idea that moral reasons are not reducible to consequences, feelings, or commands — that morality has its own domain and its own authority. This remains, despite all the challenges, a defensible and important position.

The limits of theoretical reason: the demonstration that certain questions — God, soul, freedom — lie beyond what theoretical reason can resolve. This clears the ground for genuine intellectual humility about metaphysics.

And this:

The recognition that we are not passive receivers of reality — that the mind is active, that it structures experience, that who we are shapes what we can know.

This is not idealism. It is not relativism. It is the insistence that the knower is part of the story, that the question “what is reality like?” cannot be separated from “what kind of creature is asking?”

We are still living in the aftermath of that insight.

Coda

Kant died in 1804, aged seventy-nine, in Königsberg.

His last words, reportedly, were: “Es ist gut.”

It is good.

The man who had mapped the limits of human reason, who had grounded ethics in autonomy, who had argued that beauty required free play of the faculties — died with a two-word verdict on the whole enterprise.

It is good.

He never left Königsberg. He changed everything anyway.

There is a lesson in that, about the relationship between movement and thought, between travel and depth, between the size of the world you inhabit and the size of the world you make.

His afternoon walk was enough.

Further reading: Kant — Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals (trans. Christine Korsgaard) Kant — Prolegomena to Any Future Metaphysics (easier than the Critique) Roger Scruton — Kant: A Very Short Introduction Christine Korsgaard — Creating the Kingdom of Ends (1996) Sebastian Gardner — Kant and the Critique of Pure Reason (1999) For the brave: Critique of Pure Reason (trans. Norman Kemp Smith)
entry.summary: On synthetic a priori judgments, the categorical imperative, and the most boring revolutionary in the history of philosophy.
